source.title: Verify offline bundle signature during preflight (fail closed on invalid/untrusted)
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Context: As part of the Redwood Private on-prem/offline bundle signing initiative, the installer must verify bundle provenance before any install/upgrade steps run. This PR adds signature verification to the installer preflight and fails closed when the signature is invalid or the signing key is not trusted. This is the first enforcement point; trust-store/rotation and break-glass flows will land in follow-up PRs. What this does: - Adds a new preflight gate `verify-offline-bundle-signature` that runs after bundle discovery but before unpack/apply. - Verifies that `bundle/manifest.json` is present and canonicalizable, and that it contains a signature envelope (`signing` block) produced by the release pipeline. - Verifies the signature over the canonical manifest bytes using Ed25519 (Go stdlib `crypto/ed25519`). - Validates trust via a single pinned Redwood release public key shipped with the installer (temporary until trust store work in PR-24859). - Blocks install/upgrade on: missing signature, invalid signature, unsupported algorithm, or unknown key id. - Adds structured error codes so Support can triage consistently (see `docs/error-codes.md`). Canonicalization details (v0): - We canonicalize the manifest for verification by re-encoding JSON with stable key ordering and no insignificant whitespace. The implementation is intentionally strict and will error if unknown top-level keys are present in the `signing` block (to avoid signature bypass via ambiguous parsing). - The preflight prints the calculated manifest digest (sha256 of canonical bytes) for debugging. UX / CLI: - `redwood-private-installer preflight --bundle /path/to/bundle.tgz` now emits a clear PASS/FAIL result for signature verification. - On failure, we print: error code, reason, the expected key id (if present), and the log path. - No override flag is introduced in this PR (break-glass is a separate security decision; see ENG-4824). Testing: - Unit tests for: tampered manifest, mismatched signature, missing signing block, unknown key id. - A basic integration harness that runs preflight against a fixture bundle produced by `tools/make-test-bundle` (unsigned and signed variants). Notes / follow-ups: - Trust store + key rollover support: PR-24859. - Detached signature file layout: PR-24933. - Downgrade prevention using signed version metadata: PR-24902. - Improve error remediation messaging: PR-24873. Rollout consideration: This adds enforcement only when a bundle includes a `signing` block. For existing unsigned bundles used by customers, preflight will fail with a clear error; release engineering confirmed the next Private RC will ship signed bundles in parallel with an updated installer. Related: Linear ENG-4823 (installer preflight verification), ENG-4821 (manifest canonicalization), ENG-4822 (pipeline signing step).
commits: installer/preflight: add VerifyBundleSignatureStep and wire into default preflight pipeline
security/signing: add manifest canonicalizer + ed25519 verify helper
config: add support for pinned release public key (v0) + key id matching
docs: add preflight signature verification error codes and examples
tests: fixtures + unit tests for tamper/unknown-key/missing-signature scenarios
review_conversation: Marcus Lin (Security): The verification must fail closed on any parsing ambiguity. Please confirm canonicalization is deterministic and signature never covers a partially parsed subset of the manifest.

Ethan Park (Author): Agree. Canonicalization re-encodes the full manifest struct (excluding the `signing.signature` field) with stable ordering; any JSON that fails strict decode errors out. Added a test for unknown fields under `signing` to ensure we fail.

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): Key distribution: pinned key in binary is fine short-term, but we need a path to rotate without forcing an installer rebuild for air-gapped customers. Can you leave a seam for a trust store?
Ethan Park (Author): Added `TrustedKeyProvider` interface; default implementation returns the embedded key. Follow-up PR will add filesystem-backed trust store and support multiple keys (ENG-4825).
Sahana Iyer (Build/CI): Please avoid depending on non-standard JSON libs if possible. Also ensure any test fixtures are generated deterministically.

Ethan Park (Author): Using stdlib JSON + a small canonical encoder; fixtures generated via `tools/make-test-bundle` with fixed timestamps in tests. Removed an earlier dependency on a third-party canonical JSON package.
Sean Gallagher (SRE): Can we log a single-line summary for preflight results and include the manifest digest? This will help when customers paste logs into tickets.
Ethan Park (Author): Added a summary line: `preflight.verify_bundle_signature: PASS key_id=... manifest_sha256=...` and mirrored it on failure with an error code.
Marcus Lin (Security) - Requested changes: Ensure we reject signatures with unsupported alg, and do not accept missing key id (prevents confusing trust selection).
Ethan Park (Author): Implemented explicit allowlist (`ed25519` only for v0) and require `key_id` to be set; updated tests accordingly.
Connor O'Brien (Release Eng) - Approval: Looks good for phase 1. Please link this PR from the release gating checklist once merged.
Marcus Lin (Security) - Approval: Approved with follow-up required for trust store and key rotation.
Sean Gallagher (SRE) - Approval: Good to merge.
notable_ci_events: 2025-11-22: integration tests failed (non-deterministic fixture timestamp); fixed by forcing UTC + fixed `SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH` in test bundle generator; rerun passed.
2025-11-25: lint failure (unused error var in verify step); fixed in squash.
release_notes: Installer now verifies offline bundle signatures during preflight and blocks install/upgrade when signature is invalid or signing key is untrusted.
Preflight output includes manifest SHA-256 digest and structured error codes for Support triage.
related_links: Linear: ENG-4823 - Installer preflight verification and fail-closed behavior
Linear: ENG-4821 - Manifest schema + canonicalization rules
Follow-up: PR-24859 - Trust store and key rollover support

source.title: Selective component vendoring and federated-registry seed tool for air-gapped installs
source.author: Evelyn Cho
source.description:
Motivation: customers running Redwood Private in highly constrained air-gapped sites asked for smaller, auditable offline bundles and a reproducible way to seed multiple local registries (federated seed) when performing staged rollouts across disconnected clusters. This PR introduces a selective component vendoring strategy and a small, focused registry-seed CLI that can bootstrap multiple registries from a shrinkwrapped manifest.

What this changes:
- Selective component vendoring: a new manifest layer (component-shrinkwrap.yaml) allows operators to opt-in to vendor only the runtime components they need (kubelet, containerd, cni, crictl, kernel-modules) instead of the entire distro image set. The installer will generate slim OCI bundles accordingly, reducing offline bundle size by up to ~60% in sample overlays.
- Federated registry seed tool: a new binary (installer/cmd/seed-registry) that reads a vendored index and performs concurrent, resumable pushes to multiple local registries with content verification and checksum reconciliation. It supports HTTP registry proxies and atomic promotion tags for staged rollouts.
- Terraform module tweaks: updates to terraform/modules/registry to expose per-component credentials and a optional bootstrap lifecycle that integrates with the seed tool.
- Helm hooks and charts: installer/charts/airgap tweaks to add a lightweight preflight Job that validates the presence + signatures of vendored components and a post-upgrade hook that can trigger an idempotent registry reconciliation.

Migration notes: existing customers using full-bundle installation are unaffected by default. To adopt selective vendoring, run the new `redwood-installer generate-shrinkwrap --components kubelet,containerd,cni` to create a component-shrinkwrap.yaml and then build an overlay. The seed tool requires a credentials file in the registry module (see docs).

Testing and validation:
- Unit: new unit tests cover shrinkwrap parsing, component selection heuristics, and OCI index generation.
- Integration: ran seed tool against a 3-registry federated testbed (minio-backed registries) and validated image checksums and promotion behavior under simulated network interruptions.
- E2E: smoke install against K3s and RKE2 variants with selective vendoring enabled.

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: operator misconfiguration may produce bundles missing required kernel modules for certain distros. Mitigation: installer will run a compatibility check and include a per-distro required-component whitelist; the preflight Job blocks install if critical components are missing.
- Risk: concurrent pushes to registries can be throttled by very small airgap appliances. Mitigation: seed tool exposes concurrency and retry knobs and defaults to conservative values when target registries report latency.

Rollout plan: merge to main; stage as a minor opt-in feature in the next nightly release; collect telemetry from opt-in installs and iterate.

Checklist: 
- [x] implementation
- [x] unit & integration tests
- [x] docs (installer/docs/component-vendoring.md and registry-seed.md)
- [x] changelog entry

Notes: This PR focuses on operational ergonomics for constrained sites rather than multi-arch duplication. Multi-arch vendoring will be left to a follow-up where we integrate delta indexing and cross-arch promotion.
commits: c1a5b9: scaffold selective vendoring manifest and parsing
4d2f21: add federated registry seed CLI with resumable push logic
9b3efe: terraform registry module: add component_credentials and bootstrap lifecycle
f7a11c: helm chart: add preflight signature check job and post-upgrade registry-reconcile hook
2dc8a0: add unit/integration tests and docs
changed_files: Makefile: updates to build seed-registry target
review_comments: Priya Natarajan: Could we add an explicit compatibility matrix for kernel modules per distro? (requested change)
Evelyn Cho: Added a per-distro required-component whitelist and documented it under component-vendoring.md. See latest commit f7a11c.
Liam O'Connor: Seed tool looks good; please add granular backoff/retry config and a note about TLS certs for private registries. (requested change)
Evelyn Cho: Added concurrency and retry knobs, and a TLS cert path option. Included examples in registry-seed.md.
Mateo Ruiz: Integration tests flaked once under simulated network drop; can you make the default concurrency lower for low-end appliances? (suggestion)
Evelyn Cho: Lowered default concurrency to 2 and added an environment override. Reran integration tests; all green.
source.release_notes: Added selective component vendoring and a federated registry seed tool for air-gapped installs. Operators can now generate smaller offline bundles by vendoring only required runtime components and use the seed CLI to bootstrap multiple local registries with atomic promotion tags. Opt-in feature; consult component-vendoring.md for migration steps.
source.test_plan: Run `make test` for unit tests, `go test ./... -run TestSeedTool` for integration. Manual E2E: generate a shrinkwrap targeting kubelet+containerd, build overlay, seed two local registries using seed-registry with TLS certs, and perform a staged install on RKE2. Verify checksum match and promotion behavior.
source.upgrade_notes: No automatic upgrade required. For teams adopting selective vendoring: generate a shrinkwrap and rebuild the offline bundle; use the seed tool to populate target registries before running the installer. To rollback, use the registry's atomic tags to revert to previous promoted tag and re-run installer with --force-reconcile if necessary.

source.title: operator-image-pinning-and-postrollback-validation-hooks
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds operator image digest pinning in installer manifests and terraform, chart sub-release stamps, and a Helm post-rollback validation hook to detect data-plane regressions after rollback. Includes docs and e2e upgrade test updates.
source.description:
Motivation: In several recent customer upgrade exercises we observed instability caused by operator images floating between upgrades and by silent chart metadata drift that allowed incompatible post-rollback states. This PR introduces three complementary safeguards: (1) operator image pinning via digest lockfiles surfaced in both the installer manifests and Terraform module outputs; (2) a chart sub-release stamping mechanism so the installer can detect and refuse upgrades that change sub-release semantics without an explicit migration plan; (3) a Helm post-rollback validation hook that runs lightweight data-plane health checks and fails the rollback (leaving operator in quarantined mode) if core invariants are violated.

What I changed (summary): Added an image lockfile generator and validation flow to the installer CLI; extended terraform/modules/installer to accept and export image_digest values and refuse plan/apply when the lockfile is stale; modified Helm charts to include a chartSubRelease annotation and collector logic in the operator bootstrap; added a post-rollback Helm hook that executes a small job which runs sanity checks (DB schema version, KV cache presence, background worker connectivity). Added docs: upgrade-runbook and operator-image-lockfile guide. Added e2e upgrade job to CI that runs a staged upgrade -> rollback -> validation flow to exercise the new hooks. Implemented a small migration guard in the installer that annotates resources with schemaVersion markers and will block a downgrade if the on-disk schema version is greater than the target release.

Commit highlights (high level): - add: installer/cmd/lockfile: generator + validator - add: terraform/modules/installer/image-lock.tf + outputs - add: helm/templates/hooks/post-rollback-validate.yaml - change: helm/Chart.yaml (new chartSubRelease field and annotation processing) - change: installer/upgrade/guard: schema-version preflight checks - add: docs/upgrade-runbook.md and docs/operator-image-lockfile.md - add: ci/e2e/upgrade-rollback-flow.yaml - tests: integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go and unit tests for lockfile validation

Files changed (high level): helm/Chart.yaml, helm/values.schema.json, helm/templates/hooks/post-rollback-validate.yaml, installer/cmd/lockfile/*.go, installer/upgrade/guard.go, terraform/modules/installer/image-lock.tf, docs/upgrade-runbook.md, docs/operator-image-lockfile.md, ci/e2e/upgrade-rollback-flow.yaml, integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go, tests/fixtures/*, scripts/bin/lockfile-generator.sh

Implementation notes: - Image pinning: the installer generates a lockfile mapping component -> image@sha256:digest. The terraform module consumes a lockfile path and exposes image_digest outputs so upgrades pin the operator/controller image. During a dry-run the installer will fail if current cluster images differ from lockfile (opt-in override supported for emergency operations). - Chart sub-release stamping: Chart.yaml gains a new optional field chartSubRelease. The operator bootstraps will compare stored sub-release (in ConfigMap metadata) with the incoming chartSubRelease and require an explicit migration when the stamp increases in a way that implies structural change. - Post-rollback validation: Implemented as a Helm post-rollback hook that triggers a short-lived pod which runs a small binary (included in operator image) to validate DB schema, consumer queue health, and cached KV consistency. If validation fails the hook marks the release with label rollbackValidation=failed and operator enters quarantine mode and emits a high-severity event. - Downgrade guard: Installer upgrade path now sets a schemaVersion marker on the cluster; the preflight will fail if targetVersion < schemaVersion and the user has not supplied --allow-downgrade with an explicit migration plan.

Testing & CI: Unit tests for lockfile parsing/validation (installer tests), integration harness that runs a create-upgrade-rollback scenario (integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go), and a new e2e pipeline job runs a small cluster in KinD that exercises the lockfile + post-rollback hook path. CI summary: initial run failed due to flaky timing in the hook job; fixed by adding a 30s readiness probe and retry loop. Final CI: all checks pass.

Migration plan & rollout: This PR is safe to merge to main but requires a coordinated rollout: - Step 1 (canary): merge and enable image lockfile generation for internal staging only; do not enforce failure on stale lockfile (log-only). - Step 2: enable enforcement for beta clusters with a migration-runbook run. - Step 3: enable enforcement by default and surface upgrade failures via Console. For private deployments customers can opt-in to enforcement by supplying the lockfile to the terraform module.

Backwards compatibility: default behavior is non-breaking (lockfile generation and chartSubRelease are optional). Enabling enforcement is opt-in until Step 3 described above. Downgrade guard is conservative and requires explicit --allow-downgrade to proceed.

Risks & mitigations: - Risk: operator image digests drift causing unexpected failures for customers who rely on image tags. Mitigation: we provide an automated lockfile generator and the installer supports a staged rollout with an opt-in enforcement flag. - Risk: post-rollback hook could cause longer rollback times. Mitigation: validation is intentionally lightweight (<60s typical) and can be disabled per-release via annotation for emergency rollbacks.

Related work: previous PRs added manifest hashes and version lockfiles; this complements them by publishing digest pins, adding chart sub-release semantics, and automating validation after rollback. See related Linear items: ENG-5124 (upgrade safety), ENG-5178 (helm rollback validation).

Notes for reviewers: focus on the lockfile format, terraform consumption surface, and the failure semantics of the post-rollback hook. The hook uses the operator image binary (validate-bridge) which already exists in the image; integration tests exercise failure and success paths.

Merge outcome: squash-merged into main after approvals and CI green.
review_conversation: Diego Alvarez (reviewer): Please add a test for the terraform module that asserts image_digest output is populated when a lockfile is provided.
Maya Chen (author): Added terraform unit test and a small fixture; pushed patch to generator to expose the imageDigest output.
Priya Natarajan (reviewer): The post-rollback job looked flaky in the first CI run; can we add a retry loop and a readiness probe?
Maya Chen (author): Added a 30s readiness probe and a 3-retry loop in the hook job; updated integration test to account for backoff.
Samir Patel (reviewer): We should avoid failing customer rollbacks by default. Ensure enforcement is opt-in and documented.
Maya Chen (author): Enforcement is opt-in until the staged rollout completes; clarified in docs and added --enforce-lockfile flag to the installer.
source.additional_notes: This PR intentionally keeps enforcement opt-in to avoid surprise failures in customer environments. Operations runbook and console messaging will be published in a follow-up that adds UI toggles for enforcement state.

Files changed (high level): helm/Chart.yaml, helm/values.schema.json, helm/templates/hooks/post-rollback-validate.yaml, installer/cmd/lockfile/*.go, installer/upgrade/guard.go, terraform/modules/installer/image-lock.tf, docs/upgrade-runbook.md, docs/operator-image-lockfile.md, ci/e2e/upgrade-rollback-flow.yaml, integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go, tests/fixtures/*, scripts/bin/lockfile-generator.sh

Implementation notes: - Image pinning: the installer generates a lockfile mapping component -> image@sha256:digest. The terraform module consumes a lockfile path and exposes image_digest outputs so upgrades pin the operator/controller image. During a dry-run the installer will fail if current cluster images differ from lockfile (opt-in override supported for emergency operations). - Chart sub-release stamping: Chart.yaml gains a new optional field chartSubRelease. The operator bootstraps will compare stored sub-release (in ConfigMap metadata) with the incoming chartSubRelease and require an explicit migration when the stamp increases in a way that implies structural change. - Post-rollback validation: Implemented as a Helm post-rollback hook that triggers a short-lived pod which runs a small binary (included in operator image) to validate DB schema, consumer queue health, and cached KV consistency. If validation fails the hook marks the release with label rollbackValidation=failed and operator enters quarantine mode and emits a high-severity event. - Downgrade guard: Installer upgrade path now sets a schemaVersion marker on the cluster; the preflight will fail if targetVersion < schemaVersion and the user has not supplied --allow-downgrade with an explicit migration plan.

Testing & CI: Unit tests for lockfile parsing/validation (installer tests), integration harness that runs a create-upgrade-rollback scenario (integration/upgrade_rollback_test.go), and a new e2e pipeline job runs a small cluster in KinD that exercises the lockfile + post-rollback hook path. CI summary: initial run failed due to flaky timing in the hook job; fixed by adding a 30s readiness probe and retry loop. Final CI: all checks pass.

Migration plan & rollout: This PR is safe to merge to main but requires a coordinated rollout: - Step 1 (canary): merge and enable image lockfile generation for internal staging only; do not enforce failure on stale lockfile (log-only). - Step 2: enable enforcement for beta clusters with a migration-runbook run. - Step 3: enable enforcement by default and surface upgrade failures via Console. For private deployments customers can opt-in to enforcement by supplying the lockfile to the terraform module.

Backwards compatibility: default behavior is non-breaking (lockfile generation and chartSubRelease are optional). Enabling enforcement is opt-in until Step 3 described above. Downgrade guard is conservative and requires explicit --allow-downgrade to proceed.

Risks & mitigations: - Risk: operator image digests drift causing unexpected failures for customers who rely on image tags. Mitigation: we provide an automated lockfile generator and the installer supports a staged rollout with an opt-in enforcement flag. - Risk: post-rollback hook could cause longer rollback times. Mitigation: validation is intentionally lightweight (<60s typical) and can be disabled per-release via annotation for emergency rollbacks.

Related work: previous PRs added manifest hashes and version lockfiles; this complements them by publishing digest pins, adding chart sub-release semantics, and automating validation after rollback. See related Linear items: ENG-5124 (upgrade safety), ENG-5178 (helm rollback validation).

Notes for reviewers: focus on the lockfile format, terraform consumption surface, and the failure semantics of the post-rollback hook. The hook uses the operator image binary (validate-bridge) which already exists in the image; integration tests exercise failure and success paths.

Merge outcome: squash-merged into main after approvals and CI green.
text:
Motivation: In several recent customer upgrade exercises we observed instability caused by operator images floating between upgrades and by silent chart metadata drift that allowed incompatible post-rollback states. This PR introduces three complementary safeguards: (1) operator image pinning via digest lockfiles surfaced in both the installer manifests and Terraform module outputs; (2) a chart sub-release stamping mechanism so the installer can detect and refuse upgrades that change sub-release semantics without an explicit migration plan; (3) a Helm post-rollback validation hook that runs lightweight data-plane health checks and fails the rollback (leaving operator in quarantined mode) if core invariants are violated.

What I changed (summary): Added an image lockfile generator and validation flow to the installer CLI; extended terraform/modules/installer to accept and export image_digest values and refuse plan/apply when the lockfile is stale; modified Helm charts to include a chartSubRelease annotation and collector logic in the operator bootstrap; added a post-rollback Helm hook that executes a small job which runs sanity checks (DB schema version, KV cache presence, background worker connectivity). Added docs: upgrade-runbook and operator-image-lockfile guide. Added e2e upgrade job to CI that runs a staged upgrade -> rollback -> validation flow to exercise the new hooks. Implemented a small migration guard in the installer that annotates resources with schemaVersion markers and will block a downgrade if the on-disk schema version is greater than the target release.

source.title: Add KMS preflight validation, permission checks, and audit-friendly validation report
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description: Context: We keep seeing Private installs fail late (or worse: succeed but later decrypt fails) due to KMS misconfiguration: wrong key ARN/region, missing IAM actions, key disabled, policy doesn’t allow the installer principal, or customer KMS throttling. This PR adds an explicit KMS preflight step to the Private installer so we fail fast with actionable output, and we produce an audit-friendly validation report that customers can attach to change tickets/security evidence. Summary of changes (high level): 1) New installer preflight stage: "kms" runs by default when KMS envelope encryption is configured (and can also be invoked explicitly via `redwood-private-installer validate kms`). 2) Permission + configuration checks with actionable remediation: resolves KMS key identifier, validates region alignment, validates key state (enabled, not pending deletion), and performs a safe Encrypt/Decrypt round-trip using a short-lived random payload (never persisted). The checks are mapped into a small set of installer error codes and messages that line up with the platform-wide KMS error taxonomy (AccessDenied, NotFound, Throttling, Timeout, InvalidCiphertext, DisabledKey). 3) Audit/ops report generation: on every run (success or failure), write a structured JSON report to `./reports/kms-preflight-<timestamp>.json` (override via `--report-path`). Report includes: installer version/build, timestamp, deployment id/cluster name (if available), cloud provider, key identifier (redacted by default), principal ARN/subject (redacted by default), each check result, latency per call, AWS request IDs (or equivalent), and a top-level pass/fail summary. Sensitive fields are excluded (no plaintext, no ciphertext, no auth headers, no full policy documents). 4) Guardrails: `--skip-kms-preflight` remains available for break-glass/airgapped bootstrap flows, but now requires `--i-understand-kms-risk` and emits a prominent warning in stdout and the report. 5) Support ergonomics: error output now includes “what we tried”, “what failed”, and “how to fix” sections with copy/paste IAM action lists, plus a hint to attach the report file when opening a support ticket. Implementation notes: - AWS KMS: uses DescribeKey + Encrypt/Decrypt round-trip. We intentionally do NOT call GenerateDataKey to avoid requiring broader permissions than runtime decrypt/encrypt needs in some deployments; the installer validates the minimum runtime envelope path. - Timeouts/retries: preflight uses conservative timeouts (2s per call, max 2 retries w/ jitter) to avoid long hangs during install. Throttling is reported clearly with suggested next steps (quota/limit, backoff, or allowlist of installer IP if applicable). - Redaction: by default key ARN is truncated to last 12 chars, principal is truncated to account + role name; `--report-include-identifiers` will include full identifiers if the operator chooses (still excludes secrets). - Output format stability: report JSON includes a `schema_version` field for future parsing by audit pipelines. Testing: - Unit tests for redaction, error mapping, and report serialization. - A small mocked KMS client harness that simulates AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/DisabledKey and asserts installer output and report contents. - CI adds a smoke run for `validate kms` on a stubbed config (no real cloud creds) to ensure the stage wiring doesn’t regress. Operator-facing behavior (examples): - Missing permission -> installer fails with: "KMS preflight failed: AccessDenied on Decrypt. Ensure the installer principal has kms:Decrypt on <key>. See report: reports/kms-preflight-...json" and includes a minimal IAM snippet. - Wrong region -> installer fails with: "KMS key region mismatch: key is in us-west-2 but deployment configured for us-east-1". - Throttling -> installer fails with: "KMS throttling detected (429/ThrottlingException). Reduce parallelism, check KMS quotas, or retry later. See report for request IDs and latencies." Checklist: [x] Backwards compatible (new preflight only runs when KMS is enabled; skip flag available) [x] Report contains no secrets and is safe to attach to change tickets [x] Error messages include remediation hints [x] Added tests for key failure modes. Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Hook report export into the broader "evidence pack" generation flow (see ENG-4826). - Add GCP/Azure native KMS provider implementations in installer (today we validate AWS KMS path; other providers remain config-validated only).
commits: installer: add KMS preflight stage wiring + CLI entrypoint
kms: implement AWS KMS validator (DescribeKey + Encrypt/Decrypt round-trip)
installer: add structured report writer with schema_version + redaction helpers
installer: map KMS failures to canonical taxonomy + actionable messages
tests: add mocked KMS harness for AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttle/Timeout/DisabledKey
docs: update installer output hints for attaching KMS preflight report to support tickets
ci_checks: installer smoke (validate kms w/ mocked client): pass
review_conversation: Hanae Suzuki (Security): Please confirm the report cannot leak plaintext/ciphertext or full key policies. Also prefer redaction-on by default. Author: Confirmed; report excludes plaintext/ciphertext entirely and does not fetch policy documents. Default is redaction-on, with an explicit `--report-include-identifiers` override.

Jordan Lee (Policy): The remediation hint should list the minimum IAM actions required. Also clarify that installer and runtime roles can be different. Author: Added per-check IAM action lists (DescribeKey, Encrypt, Decrypt) and a note in output/report that installer principal may differ from runtime principal; the preflight validates the principal actually executing the installer.

Sean Gallagher (SRE): Watch out for long installs if KMS is degraded; keep retry budgets tight and include request IDs for provider escalation. Author: Set 2s per-call timeout, 2 retries w/ jitter, and included request IDs + per-call latency in the report. Also added explicit Throttling messaging.

Vivek Kulkarni (Private Deployments): We need a break-glass path for airgapped bootstrap but it must be loud. Author: Kept `--skip-kms-preflight` but required `--i-understand-kms-risk` and wrote the skip decision into the report.

Kira Thompson (Docs): Can we point operators to attach the report to support tickets? Author: Added a standard footer line to failure output and to the report header: “Attach this file to Redwood Support for faster triage.”

source.title: Introduce KMS Federation Operator + SealedSecret Guardian for cross-cluster HSM key propagation
source.author: Lena Ortega
source.description: Context: customers deploying Redwood Private across multiple air-gapped datacenters requested automated, auditable propagation of HSM-wrapped keys and TLS certs between primary KMS instances and read-only local KMS proxies. This PR introduces a KMS Federation Operator (KFO) plus a SealedSecret Guardian daemonset to orchestrate safe key material replication, reconciled by Terraform/Helm hooks. Motivation: avoid manual sealed secret unseal workflows during cluster join, reduce time-to-recovery for HSM failovers, and provide an auditable policy-based propagation model. Scope: operator CRDs + controller, Helm chart changes (upgrade hooks, post-install job), Terraform module for federation resources, upgrade/rollback safe hooks, docs and test harness. Checklist: [x] CRD and controller implementation; [x] Helm templates and upgrade-hook jobs; [x] Terraform module to provision federation roles and KMS ACLs; [x] Migration job (idempotent) to materialize existing sealed secrets into guardian-managed stores; [x] E2E smoke test in perf-canary; [x] Docs/upgrade guide and rollback notes. Design notes: - KFO implements a controller that watches KMSFederation CR and ensures lockless, nonce-based propagation of HSM-wrapped KEKs to target clusters via sealed secrets. - SealedSecret Guardian runs with minimal privileges; it consumes Vault/Unseal operators only for bootstrap and stores encrypted blobs in sealedsecret CRs. - Propagation policy allows white/blacklists of key IDs and optional TTL-based refreshing. - Operator uses a confirmation handshake (signed nonce) to prevent replay attacks; unit tests cover nonce lifecycle and on-disk rotated key caches. Backwards compatibility: migration job will no-op if cluster already managed by existing rotation tooling. Rollback strategy: helm pre-delete hook removes Guardian with a drain window; federation CRs are annotated with previous-state snapshots to allow safe rollback. Security review: keys never reside unwrapped on disk; all operations require KMS delegated roles one-time tokens. See SUP-4517 for customer repro.
commits: feat(kfo): add CRD for KMSFederation and initial controller scaffolding
feat(guardian): sealedsecret guardian daemonset with bootstrap flow and nonce handshake
chore(helm): add kfo chart, upgrade-hook job and rbac bindings
feat(terraform): kms-federation module to create roles, policies, and federation endpoints
docs: upgrade guide and migration playbook for existing installations
test: add e2e smoke to perf-canary and unit tests for nonce handling
fix(ci): increase integration test timeout and add retry loop for Vault readiness
review_threads: Marcus Patel (review): Security: how do we ensure the one-time token cannot be replayed? Please add unit tests for token expiry handling.
Author (reply - Lena Ortega): Added nonce lifecycle tests and token expiry verification; controller now records token nonces in an in-memory LRU and emits metrics.
Ava Chen (review): Helm upgrade hook should be idempotent for operator restart during upgrade. Also add pre-delete drain window for guardian.
Author (reply): Upgrade-job made idempotent; pre-delete drain added with configurable timeout (default 60s).
Diego Navarro (approval): Verified e2e smoke on perf-canary and manual HSM switchover; leaving approval after test artifacts uploaded.
ci_checks: integration-tests: pass after 2nd run (Vault readiness flaky on first run)
source.merge_outcome: merged into main after CI green and approvals
source.release_notes: Adds KMS Federation Operator and SealedSecret Guardian to automate HSM-wrapped key propagation across clusters. Includes Helm chart, Terraform module, migration job, and upgrade/rollback guidance. Customers should follow docs/upgrade-guides/kms-federation.md for on-prem migration steps.

Scope: operator CRDs + controller, Helm chart changes (upgrade hooks, post-install job), Terraform module for federation resources, upgrade/rollback safe hooks, docs and test harness. Checklist: [x] CRD and controller implementation; [x] Helm templates and upgrade-hook jobs; [x] Terraform module to provision federation roles and KMS ACLs; [x] Migration job (idempotent) to materialize existing sealed secrets into guardian-managed stores; [x] E2E smoke test in perf-canary; [x] Docs/upgrade guide and rollback notes. Design notes: - KFO implements a controller that watches KMSFederation CR and ensures lockless, nonce-based propagation of HSM-wrapped KEKs to target clusters via sealed secrets. - SealedSecret Guardian runs with minimal privileges; it consumes Vault/Unseal operators only for bootstrap and stores encrypted blobs in sealedsecret CRs. - Propagation policy allows white/blacklists of key IDs and optional TTL-based refreshing. - Operator uses a confirmation handshake (signed nonce) to prevent replay attacks; unit tests cover nonce lifecycle and on-disk rotated key caches. Backwards compatibility: migration job will no-op if cluster already managed by existing rotation tooling. Rollback strategy: helm pre-delete hook removes Guardian with a drain window; federation CRs are annotated with previous-state snapshots to allow safe rollback. Security review: keys never reside unwrapped on disk; all operations require KMS delegated roles one-time tokens. See SUP-4517 for customer repro.

source.title: vendor rke2 CNI plugins + offline upgrade/rollback hooks for air-gapped installs
source.author: Leah Park
source.description: Motivation: Customers running Redwood Private on-prem with RKE2 often operate fully air-gapped clusters and require a bundled, validated CNI set plus deterministic upgrade/rollback behavior during offline upgrades. This PR vendors a minimal set of RKE2-compatible CNI artifacts (canal/flannel + calico dataplane bits and rke2-cni shim) into the offline bundle, adds a small SELinux module and policy packaging step required for RHEL environments, and introduces an idempotent offline upgrade/rollback hook framework so operators can perform safe rollbacks without internet access. What changed (high-level): 1) Packaging: added vendor/cni/ directory with compressed tarballs for canal and calico dataplane, rke2-cni shim, and SHA256 checksums. 2) Installer runtime: extend airgap preflight to verify CNI artifact signatures and space checks, and to unpack only the CNI payload needed by the target kube-distro. 3) Hooks: new hooks/upgrade-preflight.sh, hooks/upgrade-postinstall.sh, hooks/rollback.sh that run entirely offline; hooks implement graceful drain/uncordon, KV cache preservation for Redwood services, and automated iptables/ebtables cleanup. 4) SELinux: small rpm-style policy bundle and a conditional module loader used only on RHEL/OL family hosts. 5) Helm and manifests: a patched charts/redwood-system chart to allow using local image/asset references for CNI DaemonSet and to expose a cni.install.strategy value (bundle|host). 6) Tests & docs: integration test harness updates and an ops-guide section documenting offline upgrade/rollback commands and failure modes. Commits summary: - add vendor/cni payloads and checksums - implement offline preflight and unpack logic - add selinux module packaging and loader - add offline hook framework and idempotent rollback script - patch helm chart to accept local cni references - add integration tests and docs Notes and rationale: - We picked canal (flannel+calico) and a trimmed calico dataplane set because they cover the networking/mtu/ipam modes used by most customers and keep the vendor bundle size reasonable. - The rollback hook is intentionally conservative: it first attempts a fast rollback using Helm release history where available, then falls back to manual manifest restore using a timestamped snapshot kept in /var/lib/redwood/installer/snapshots. - SELinux module is optional at runtime; installer will skip and emit a warning on distros that don't support semodule. Files changed (high-level): - vendor/cni/canal-1.14.0.tar.gz.sha256 - vendor/cni/canal-1.14.0.tar.gz - vendor/cni/calico-dataplane-3.25.0.tar.gz - installer/preflight/checks_cni.go (new checks) - installer/runtime/unpack_airgap.go (unpack selector changes) - hooks/upgrade-preflight.sh - hooks/upgrade-postinstall.sh - hooks/rollback.sh - packaging/selinux/redwood_cni.pp - charts/redwood-system/values.yaml (cni.install.strategy) - charts/redwood-system/templates/cni-daemonset.yaml (local image refs) - integration/tests/airgap_cni_upgrade_test.sh - docs/ops/offline-upgrades.md Testing performed: - Local RKE2 clusters (v1.25 and v1.26) in air-gapped VM lab: full upgrade flow (install → upgrade → rollback) exercised end-to-end on Ubuntu 22.04 and RHEL 9. - SELinux module load/unload tested on RHEL 9.1. - CI: unit tests + integration script run in a simulated airgap (--local-bundle flag). Observed CI was flaky initially due to checksum handling; fixed by switching to streaming SHA256 verification and re-running pipelines. Security considerations: - All vendored artifacts are accompanied by SHA256 checksums and an internal GPG signature (keys stored in the private installer signing keyset). The installer enforces signature verification by default for air-gapped installs; operators can pass --insecure for lab usage. - The SELinux policy is minimal and scoped to the installer and CNI sockets; no broad permissions granted. Upgrade/rollback operator steps (short): 1) Place offline bundle on installer host and run ./install.sh --bundle /path/to/bundle --kube-distro rke2 2) To upgrade: ./installerctl upgrade --bundle /t/bundle --hooks enable 3) To rollback: ./installerctl rollback --to-snapshot 2025-11-18--21:04 4) If automatic rollback fails, manual restore path documented in docs/ops/offline-upgrades.md Related Linear/Jira: ENG-7421 (airgap CNI vendoring), ENG-7530 (rollback hooks), PM-1182 (ops guide). Release notes: - Air-gapped installs: vendor canal+calico dataplane for RKE2; verify artifacts offline - Installer will now expose cni.install.strategy and honor local CNI references for air-gapped clusters - Add offline upgrade/rollback hooks for safer on-prem upgrades - SELinux module packaging for RHEL-based hosts Known limitations: - Bundle size increases by ~120MB. We plan to move to delta artifact delivery in a follow-up (ENG-7689). - Currently validated for RKE2 and kubeadm; K3s support requires a follow-up patch to handle k3s-specific CNI shims. Merge and rollout: Squash-merged to main after approvals from Arman and Priya and passing CI. Backported docs and a brief ops note were included in the release checklist.
commits: add vendor/cni canal and calico archives with checksums
implement airgap unpack selector and verification
add selinux policy packaging and loader
add offline hook scripts (preflight/postinstall/rollback)
patch helm chart for local cni references and values
add integration tests and docs
review_comments: Arman Singh: Left a couple of suggestions on the unpack selector — prefer explicit distro detection vs heuristic. Also flagged an edge case around partial unpack on low-disk nodes.
Leah Park: Addressed Arman's unpack selector comments; added a disk-space preflight and a stream-verify step to avoid partial unpacked artifacts.
Priya Natarajan: Approved after SELinux module scope trimmed and docs updated to call out the optional flag.
Samir Patel: CI flaky on first run due to checksum streaming; re-run fixed and I validated integration test logs.
release_notes: Vendor canal+calico CNI artifacts in the offline bundle for RKE2 air-gapped installs.
Add offline upgrade and rollback hooks to the installer; safe rollback uses Helm history or snapshot restore.
Introduce optional SELinux module packaging for RHEL hosts to avoid runtime denials.
Expose charts.redwood-system.cni.install.strategy to select bundle vs host install behavior.

source.title: add-central-log-shipper-and-dashboard-migration-tooling-for-private-installs
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Adds a centralized log shipping option (promtail -> S3 -> loki indexer) as an optional subchart to the private-installer Helm bundle, a terraform module to provision long-term object storage and Grafana workspace configuration, and a one-shot dashboard migration utility that performs a schema-safe migration of existing Grafana dashboards during upgrades. Motivation: customers deploying Private need a reliable, upgrade-safe path to connect cluster logs and persisted dashboards into the observability stack without manual intervention. This PR implements the Helm templates and values schema, the Terraform module that creates an S3-compatible bucket with lifecycle rules and IAM policy, a k8s Job-based migration runner that performs dashboard diffs and backouts, unit and e2e tests for the chart and migration tool, and documentation + an upgrade checklist. The description below summarizes commits, changed areas, CI outcomes, and review thread.
source.commits: 6 commits: Add promtail subchart and values schema; Terraform: add tf module observability/s3-grafana; Grafana migration job and safe-backout script; e2e tests for chart and migration runner; docs/upgrade-checklist and examples; fix CI lint failures and retry logic
review_comments: Luis: Please ensure IAM policy generated by terraform restricts PUT only to the migration prefix and adds lifecycle rules to prevent infinite retention. Response: Updated module to accept prefix and lifecycle block, added example usage.

Priya: How will the migration handle dashboard UID changes and panel id collisions? Response: Migration runner computes a diff and will write to a backup namespace before applying; a --dry-run mode included and a safe-backout script is provided.
Owen: CI timeout on e2e test due to slow container startup. Response: increased pod readiness timeout and added retry logic to test harness; re-ran CI and all checks passed.
source.release_notes: - Optional Helm subchart for cluster log shipping (promtail -> S3 -> loki compatible indexer). - Terraform module to provision S3-compatible storage and Grafana workspace config. - Dashboard migration utility: dry-run, apply, and safe-backout modes. - Upgrade checklist and examples for Private deploys. - e2e tests for end-to-end validation.
source.notes_on_testing: Local chart unit tests via helm-unittest and schema validation added. e2e tests run in CI against a KinD cluster with mocked S3 gateway. Migration runner covered by integration test that exercises dry-run and apply paths.
source.motivation_and_context: Customers requested minimal-ops observability onboarding for Private installs that survives upgrades and keeps dashboards consistent. Prior manual steps were error-prone during rolling upgrades and large dashboard diffs caused outages. This PR reduces operational burden by automating provisioning, safe migration, and providing rollback tooling.
source.backcompat_notes: The Helm values schema is backward-compatible; new promtail subchart is opt-in via values.enablePromtail=true. Grafana dashboards are migrated only when operator flag migrator.enabled=true to avoid surprise writes.
source.risk_mitigation: Migration runs as a k8s Job with a preflight dry-run and creates time-stamped backups of dashboards. Terraform module outputs a recommended IAM policy and an example for least-privilege. All changes gated behind feature flags in installer values.
source.changelog_entry: feature: optional centralized log shipping + grafana dashboard migration for private installs
source.acknowledgements: Thanks to infra-team (Owen Li) for Terraform review, SRE (Luis Martinez) for alert rule guidance, and Platform PM (Priya Kapoor) for upgrade flow review.

source.title: ci: generate release graph and manifest validator for private-installer artifacts
source.author: Marcus Chen
source.description: Motivation: CI for private installer releases lacked a machine-readable release graph and a manifest-level validator that ties signed artifacts (images, helm charts, tf modules) back to the release manifest. This PR adds a release-graph generator and a manifest-validator stage to the private-installer CI pipeline to ensure provenance, enable safer upgrades/rollbacks, and block unsigned/unaligned manifests from publishing. Summary of changes: - new pipeline step 'build:release-graph' which produces a directed release graph (JSON) that maps artifacts -> helm chart values -> terraform module versions and includes cryptographic digests and SBOM references; graph is collated from build outputs and attestation metadata - new pipeline step 'validate:manifest' that consumes a release graph and enforces: cosign signature verification (keyless and KMS), SBOM presence, chart values checksum, and compatibility matrix checks for upgrades - small helper library under tools/release-graph that normalizes artifact metadata, computes canonical digests, and emits signatures-ready metadata for the signer job - Helm chart linting rules extended to emit a Helm manifest fingerprint (stable ordering) and surface mismatches between chart contents and release graph - Terraform module manifest extraction to include provider pins and a short policy check to prevent unsupported provider upgrades in the same release - CI orchestration changes: the validate step runs before artifact promotion and blocks publishing to the release bucket on failure - added an automated delta-rollback manifest generator which creates a minimal rollback plan from the release graph when a canary fails Why this is different: existing pipelines stamp artifacts and upload indexes, but we need a top-level release graph that can be consumed by Private control-plane for deterministic rollouts and by on-prem installers to reconcile manifests during upgrades. This PR focuses on making the release artifact a first-class, verifiable graph rather than loose indexes. Checklist: - [x] release-graph generator implemented - [x] manifest validator implemented and integrated into the CI workflow - [x] cosign verification (keyless and KMS) wired into validator - [x] Helm fingerprinting integrated - [x] Terraform module extraction implemented - [x] unit tests for tools/release-graph - [x] e2e run in staging that simulates signed artifact promotion - [x] added docs for ops on how to interpret the release graph Changes included (high level): tools/release-graph/* (new library and CLI), ci/pipeline/build-release-graph.yaml (new job), ci/pipeline/validate-manifest.yaml (new job), charts/private-installer/patches/manifest-fingerprint.yaml (helper), terraform/modules/release-metadata/README.md (docs), scripts/delta-rollback/gen-rollback.sh (new helper), tests/integration/release-graph-e2e (e2e harness). Testing and verification: - unit coverage for release-graph normalization functions - integration test that generates a release graph from staged artifacts, signs them with a test KMS key, then runs validator (CI job uses real cosign verification in staging) - staging rollout run that exercised the delta-rollback generator after a simulated canary failure - CI history shows two failed validator runs during early iteration (missing SBOMs) which were fixed by the chart/lint rule update Rollout plan: - merged to main and enabled in staging for one week; manual gating for production release (ops approval required) - enable automatic blocking on validator failures for subsequent releases; until then validator failures are opt-in via release-note flag Migration notes: - existing release index consumers must be updated to prefer release-graph.json; a small compatibility shim is provided under tools/release-graph/bin/compatify - operators must ensure cosign public key or KMS access is available in the Private control-plane to verify signatures Security considerations: - cosign key usage requires KMS scopes; the validator supports both keyless (OIDC) verification and KMS-backed verification; by default validator rejects unsigned artifacts - SBOM presence is mandatory for private installer releases; policy can be relaxed via a per-customer policy file if needed Documentation: - docs/private-installer/release-graph.md added explaining format and how to use the delta-rollback generator - ops playbook updated with steps to manually validate a release graph Known limitations & follow-ups: - release-graph currently serializes all digests in SHA256; we will add multi-algo support (ENG-6780 follow-up) - we do not yet have an automated remediation when validator fails (ENG-6723 will track a retry/auto-fix job) Related: ENG-6723 (release graph spec), ENG-6780 (multi-algo hashing), SUP-3048 (support ticket where customer asked for deterministic rollback plans).
commits: a1b2c3d add tools/release-graph CLI and core normalization
b2c3d4e add CI job build-release-graph.yaml and outputs to staging bucket
c3d4e5f implement manifest validator with cosign verification and SBOM checks
d4e5f6a integrate helm fingerprinting and terraform extraction
e5f6a7b add delta-rollback generator and e2e test harness
f6a7b8c docs: add release-graph.md and ops playbook section
review_comments: Priya Nair: Please add a note about KMS key rotation impact on old releases; we shouldn't make old releases unverifiable.
Marcus Chen: Added rotation doc and compatibility shim for cached public keys. See tools/release-graph/compat.md
Sofia Alvarez: Validator should surface which rule failed in CI summary. Added structured failure reasons to job output.
Ethan Park: Confirmed that helm fingerprinting is order-stable across helm versions; added additional tests for templating edge cases.
Ops (comment): We need a small UX doc for operators to inspect release graphs; added docs/private-installer/release-graph.md
source.release_notes: Add release graph generation and manifest-level validation to private-installer CI. This enforces signed artifacts, SBOM presence, and compatibility checks before promoting releases. Also introduces a delta-rollback manifest generator for safer rollbacks.
footnotes: This PR intentionally keeps the release-graph format backward compatible via a small compat shim. Consumers are encouraged to migrate to release-graph.json.
Follow-ups tracked in ENG-6723 and ENG-6780.

Checklist: - [x] release-graph generator implemented - [x] manifest validator implemented and integrated into the CI workflow - [x] cosign verification (keyless and KMS) wired into validator - [x] Helm fingerprinting integrated - [x] Terraform module extraction implemented - [x] unit tests for tools/release-graph - [x] e2e run in staging that simulates signed artifact promotion - [x] added docs for ops on how to interpret the release graph Changes included (high level): tools/release-graph/* (new library and CLI), ci/pipeline/build-release-graph.yaml (new job), ci/pipeline/validate-manifest.yaml (new job), charts/private-installer/patches/manifest-fingerprint.yaml (helper), terraform/modules/release-metadata/README.md (docs), scripts/delta-rollback/gen-rollback.sh (new helper), tests/integration/release-graph-e2e (e2e harness). Testing and verification: - unit coverage for release-graph normalization functions - integration test that generates a release graph from staged artifacts, signs them with a test KMS key, then runs validator (CI job uses real cosign verification in staging) - staging rollout run that exercised the delta-rollback generator after a simulated canary failure - CI history shows two failed validator runs during early iteration (missing SBOMs) which were fixed by the chart/lint rule update Rollout plan: - merged to main and enabled in staging for one week; manual gating for production release (ops approval required) - enable automatic blocking on validator failures for subsequent releases; until then validator failures are opt-in via release-note flag Migration notes: - existing release index consumers must be updated to prefer release-graph.json; a small compatibility shim is provided under tools/release-graph/bin/compatify - operators must ensure cosign public key or KMS access is available in the Private control-plane to verify signatures Security considerations: - cosign key usage requires KMS scopes; the validator supports both keyless (OIDC) verification and KMS-backed verification; by default validator rejects unsigned artifacts - SBOM presence is mandatory for private installer releases; policy can be relaxed via a per-customer policy file if needed Documentation: - docs/private-installer/release-graph.md added explaining format and how to use the delta-rollback generator - ops playbook updated with steps to manually validate a release graph Known limitations & follow-ups: - release-graph currently serializes all digests in SHA256; we will add multi-algo support (ENG-6780 follow-up) - we do not yet have an automated remediation when validator fails (ENG-6723 will track a retry/auto-fix job) Related: ENG-6723 (release graph spec), ENG-6780 (multi-algo hashing), SUP-3048 (support ticket where customer asked for deterministic rollback plans).

This PR focuses on making the release artifact a first-class, verifiable graph rather than loose indexes. source.title: external-kms-plugin-and-cert-sync-controller-for-onprem-rotation
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: Motivation: private installations need a way to integrate external KMS (cloud or on-prem HSM gateways) without forcing Vault-as-first-class and to keep TLS artifacts synchronized across namespaces during key rotation. This PR introduces two coordinated changes: 1) an optional installer hook that deploys a lightweight external-kms-plugin sidecar and Terraform snippets to register external KMS endpoints (KMS plugin config + policy templates); 2) a cert-sync-controller (Kubernetes controller) that watches KMS-backed key metadata and existing TLS secrets (including sealed-secrets-wrapped secrets) to perform automated, atomic certificate replacements across the cluster during rotation. Goals: reduce manual rotation steps for Private deployments, support customer-managed KMS/HSM, and provide a safe upgrade path with canaries and rollbacks. The description below outlines implementation details, migration plan, and risk mitigations. Implementation summary: - Installer/terraform: new optional module `external_kms` in terraform/ modules that wires up provider endpoints, validates TLS with provided CA bundle, and outputs Kubernetes Secret manifests for connector config. - Helm: chart changes add `externalKms.enabled` flag, sidecar container image references, RBAC rules for the cert-sync-controller, and a new `preupgrade` hook to validate existing TLS assets before applying new certs. - Controller: `cert-sync-controller` (Go) runs as a Deployment; it subscribes to a KMSConfig CRD and Secret events, uses signed key fingerprints to detect rotation candidates, generates CSR via local signer when allowed, and rotates target Secrets (supports sealed-secrets encrypted payloads by unlocking via the installer-provided sealing key). - Backwards compatibility: all changes are gated behind `externalKms.enabled=false` by default; controller is opt-in. - Rollout: default canary to 5% of nodes via nodeSelector + label-based canary, automated healthchecks (liveness/readiness + smoke requests) and automatic rollback on certificate validation failures. - Testing: unit tests for controller logic, integration e2e in the benchmark-lab that exercises a simulated KMS gateway, and manual on-prem validation (steps included in docs). See release notes + upgrade instructions below. Checklist: - [x] Terraform module with input/output docs - [x] Helm chart templates and hook - [x] CRD + controller implementation - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Operator RBAC and PSP/PodSecurity verification - [x] Docs and upgrade guide - [x] Canary rollout strategy and rollback hooks Related: builds on work in ENG-4000 (vault auto-unseal) and complements sealed-secrets automation (does not replace Vault). Security: we require KMS endpoints to authenticate via mTLS or a signed token; the installer will validate CA bundles and refuses to enable the controller if authentication tests fail. For sealed-secrets, controller will only decrypt using cluster sealing key provided explicitly by the admin during install (no implicit key retrieval). Migration notes: - Upgrading from 2.3 -> 2.4: operator will detect existing TLS secrets and run a dry-run validation; admin must approve the first rotation via the console or CLI. - Rollback: chart includes helm hooks which will restore previous Secret manifests (helm rollback) and controller will stop rotating if `externalKms.enabled=false`. Observability: new metrics (redwood_cert_sync_rotations_total, redwood_cert_sync_errors, redwood_kms_plugin_sync_latency) and tracing spans (controller reconcile) added to console dashboards. Rollout plan: stage to Dedicated beta clusters for 2 weeks, then enable canary for 5% of customer on-prem clusters with customer opt-in, gather telemetry, then full release. See release_notes for more details.
commits: a1f3c6e: add terraform module external_kms with inputs and validation scripts
b4d2f9a: helm: add externalKms.enabled flag, templates for kms connector secret and preupgrade hook
c7e8128: controller: initial cert-sync-controller implementation, CRD and RBAC
d3f4a55: tests: unit tests for rotation detection + integration test against mock-kms
e9b01f2: docs: upgrade guide and security considerations
f2a9d33: tooling: add smoke check script and canary rollout helm values
review_comments: Ava Chen: Can the controller operate read-only (monitoring) mode? We should allow dry-run so customers can evaluate. -- Author: implemented a --dry-run flag and KMSConfig.dryRun boolean.
Diego Martinez: Ensure we don't leak secrets to logs during rotation. -- Author: scrubbed logs and added structured logging for event IDs only.

Priya Kapoor: How do we validate external KMS TLS bundles? Add a preflight check. -- Author: added terraform validation script and helm preupgrade hook that curls the endpoint with provided CA bundle.
Ava Chen: CI flake on e2e against mock-kms, please stabilize. -- Author: retried, added timeouts and deterministic mock responses; e2e now passes in CI.

Security review (Liam O'Neill): Verified mTLS config and sealed-secrets handling; recommended rotating the sealing key weekly for high-risk tenants. -- Author: documented recommendation in security notes.
source.release_notes:
- opt-in external KMS integration for Private installs (terraform + helm)
- cert-sync-controller to automate TLS secret rotation across namespaces (supports sealed-secrets)
- new metrics and dashboard panels for rotation observability
- upgrade notes and dry-run mode to validate before rotating

source.title: tenant-probe-injector-and-slo-synthesizer-for-private-installs
source.author: Mariana Solis
source.release_notes: Adds tenant-scoped synthetic monitoring and automated SLO/dashboard generation for private installs. Backwards compatible; opt-in via installer flags. Includes Helm and Terraform updates.
source.description:
Motivation and summary: This PR adds a tenant-aware probe injector and an SLO synthesizer to the Private Installer. The goal is to provide out-of-the-box synthetic checks and tenant-scoped SLOs/dashboards for Prometheus + Grafana in private/VPC deployments without forcing per-tenant manual configuration. This is targeted at customers who want predictable SLOs and early detection of network or configuration regressions across tenant boundaries.

What it contains: 1) A Helm subchart 'probe-injector' that deploys a small sidecar injector controller and a configurable Prometheus Blackbox Exporter + probe CRDs. The sidecar injector can attach a lightweight HTTP/TCP/ICMP probe sidecar to tenant-facing pods in the private cluster via annotations applied by the installer. 2) An 'slo-synthesizer' component (Go binary + controller) that consumes tenant intent (SLO targets stored in a namespaced ConfigMap generated by the installer or Terraform) and emits: - Prometheus recording rules and alerting rules for error and latency SLOs - Grafana dashboard JSON models per tenant with templated panels and scoreboard SLO summaries - An evaluation dashboard that aggregates tenant SLO burn rates. 3) Terraform module changes: optional probe registration resource and a new variable set to control per-tenant probe windows and scrape targets when provisioning Private instances via IaC. 4) Installer UX additions: new flags --enable-tenant-probes and --tenant-slo-defaults-file to toggle and supply defaults. 5) Upgrade/rollback safety: new canary annotations and a two-phase rollout in the Helm chart plus a migration job that can be re-run safely on rollback. 6) Tests and docs: unit tests for the synthesizer logic, integration smoke test in the installer CI to validate probe creation and rule generation, and a new docs page under docs/observability/tenant-probes.md.

Commits (high-level): - feat: add probe-injector helm subchart and CRD manifests - feat: implement slo-synthesizer controller + Go library for rule/dashboard generation - feat: add terraform module hooks for probe registration - chore: installer flags and config plumbing - ci: add integration smoke test to installer pipeline - docs: tenant probes and SLO synthesizer guide - fix: rollout hooks and rollback-safe migration job

Files changed (high level): - helm/probe-injector/* (Chart, templates, CRDs, notes) - installer/cmd/* (flags and config parsing) - installer/terraform/modules/probes/* (new module + examples) - controllers/slo-synthesizer/* (controller, rule generator, unit tests) - docs/observability/tenant-probes.md - ci/integration/installer-probe-smoke.yml - charts/redwood-private-installer/values.schema.json (new keys)

Design notes and constraints: - The injector is annotation-driven to remain opt-in; installer will annotate pods that map to tenant ingress by default when --enable-tenant-probes is set. - Probes use Blackbox Exporter to centralize probe types; exporters are deployed per-node-pool in larger installs to avoid cross-tenant noisy neighbors. - SLO synthesis uses conservative defaults (p95 latency objective, 99.9% success rate) that are documented and overrideable per tenant. - Rules and dashboards are namespaced to tenant so Grafana folder-per-tenant conventions can be kept. - We intentionally avoid storing tenant secrets in the synthesizer; it only reads ConfigMaps and emits Prometheus/Grafana objects.

Review conversation (summary): - Ethan Park (reviewer): "Can we make the probe frequency configurable per-tenant and add a safety cap to avoid overloading small clusters?" Author reply: "Yes — added terraform and installer knobs with sane defaults and a global rate-limiter in the injector. See terraform/modules/probes/README and charts notes." - Lena Fischer: "Alerting: how do we prevent duplicate alerts across tenant and cluster-level rules?" Author reply: "Synthesizer marks tenant rules with tenant labels and a `slo_scope=tenant`; cluster-level rules include `slo_scope=cluster`. Alertmanager routing is configured in a follow-up to route tenant alerts to tenant channels. Added tests to ensure no identical rule names clash across namespaces." - Omar Khan (approval after changes): "Verified rollout hooks and smoke test locally; incremental upgrade path looks safe." - CI comments: initial CI run flagged linting issues in helm templates; author fixed and re-ran. Integration smoke test initially flaky due to timing; added a readiness probe and extended wait-time in CI test.

CI status: Full pipeline green on final run (unit, helm lint, terraform fmt/validate, installer integration smoke test).

Backward compatibility & upgrade notes: - Feature is opt-in; default behavior for existing private installs is unchanged unless the new flags are set. - Migration job runs once during upgrade if tenant SLO defaults are present; it is idempotent and safe to re-run on rollback. - Helm chart bump is non-breaking and follows semantic versioning; migration steps documented in release notes.

Security & operational considerations: - The probe injector runs with minimized privileges; it only mutates pod annotations and creates probe resources in tenant namespaces. - Prometheus rules generated are limited in cardinality; synthesizer enforces label cardinality checks. - Grafana dashboard provisioning uses folder-per-tenant tokens which are rotated via the installer-managed KMS integration.

Merge outcome: Merged via squash-and-merge after approvals and green CI. Merge commit contains summarized changelog and upgrade notes.

Follow-ups (planned): - ENG-8421: PagerDuty routing for tenant SLO alerts and runbook links (linked). - SRE-309: Add cost-based probe sampling to reduce token and network usage in very large installs. - Small RFC to expose SLO targets in the Console UI for hosted parity.

Notes for release: included in next private-installer minor release; documented in release_notes and operational playbook.
