source.title: Private upgrades: backup orchestrator + snapshot hooks (CSI/EBS) + optional logical backup
source.author: Irene Choi
source.release_notes: Adds installer-managed backups for Private upgrades: pre-upgrade snapshot hooks (CSI/EBS) and optional logical DB backups with progress reporting. Persists backup metadata into upgrade state for auditability and rollback workflows. Improves failure handling with clear remediation output and a safe abort path when backups fail.
source.description: Summary: This PR adds a first-class backup orchestration phase to the Private upgrade workflow. The installer now runs a pre-upgrade backup step that can (a) create volume snapshots via pluggable snapshotters (initial support: Kubernetes CSI VolumeSnapshot and AWS EBS snapshots via CSI annotations) and/or (b) perform an optional logical DB backup (pg_dump) when snapshots are unavailable or when operators request an additional safety net. The orchestrator emits structured progress events (for CLI + future UI) and persists backup metadata into the upgrade state store so rollback and support bundles can reference what happened. Motivation: We’ve had multiple upgrade incidents/near-misses where rollback was risky because there was no deterministic backup step or because snapshot behaviors differed across environments. This change makes upgrades safe-by-default and reduces operator guesswork, aligning with ENG-4838 and the broader “Private upgrade + rollback mechanism” project. Design/behavior: 1) New phase in plan execution: pre_upgrade_backup runs after prechecks and before any control-plane schema migrations or chart changes. 2) Backup strategies: SnapshotBackup (preferred) creates snapshots for PVCs selected by label/allowlist (db, control-plane state, queue/cache if configured). Uses CSI VolumeSnapshot API if supported; falls back to an AWS EBS snapshot flow when running on EKS with the EBS CSI driver (detected via StorageClass provisioner + PV topology). LogicalBackup (optional) runs a job in-cluster to produce a compressed pg_dump stored to a configured object store endpoint or a local path in on-prem mode. The logical backup is explicitly opt-in (--backup.logical.enabled) and requires credentials/target to be present. 3) Encryption and access: The orchestrator enforces encryption expectations when possible (e.g., checks StorageClass parameters / snapshot class annotations). For logical backup targets, it validates that the destination is configured for server-side encryption when supported (S3 SSE-S3/SSE-KMS) and warns otherwise (non-blocking). 4) Progress reporting: Standardized events are emitted for start/progress/complete/failure with stable step IDs. CLI prints a compact progress view; verbose mode includes per-volume timing and snapshot IDs. 5) Failure handling: Backup failures are blocking by default (upgrade aborts before any mutations). Partial snapshot creation is treated as failure; the output includes the created snapshot IDs to help cleanup. A --backup.continue-on-failure flag exists for break-glass situations (hidden; requires confirmation prompt). 6) Auditability: The upgrade state store now records backup artifacts (snapshot IDs, VolumeSnapshot names, logical backup URI, timestamps, duration, and validation status). This is used by future rollback commands and included in support bundles (ENG-4899 follow-up will wire the bundle). Operator UX: New installer flags: --backup.enabled (default true for upgrade), --backup.strategy=snapshot|logical|snapshot+logical, --backup.snapshot.pvcSelector (label selector), --backup.logical.enabled, --backup.logical.destination (s3://… or file:///…), --backup.timeout, --backup.continue-on-failure (hidden). New commands: redwood-private upgrade plan now includes a Backup stage when enabled; redwood-private upgrade run prints a Backup section with progress. Notably, this PR does not implement restore; restore/rollback hooks will consume the recorded artifacts (ENG-4844 / PR-4862). Testing: Adds unit tests for snapshot selection, CSI capability detection, and state persistence. Adds an integration test job in CI that runs a mocked snapshotter and verifies that upgrade plan execution halts on backup failure. Limitations: On-prem snapshot support depends on the cluster’s CSI driver exposing VolumeSnapshot. If VolumeSnapshot CRDs are missing, snapshot backups will fail with a clear remediation message (install snapshot-controller + CRDs) and operators can choose logical backups instead. Logical backup currently supports Postgres only (our control-plane DB). Follow-ups: (1) Add restore hooks + verification checks for snapshots and logical backups (ENG-4838). (2) Support additional snapshot backends and explicit SnapshotClass selection (requested by design partners). (3) Wire backup metadata into support bundle and audit log events (ENG-4899 + redwood monorepo PR for audit logging).
commits: installer/backup: introduce BackupOrchestrator interface + phase wiring into upgrade executor
backup/snapshot: CSI VolumeSnapshot implementation + capability detection + PVC selection
backup/snapshot: EKS/EBS compatibility path + improved messages for unsupported StorageClass
backup/logical: pg_dump job runner + destination validation + compression + timeouts
state: persist backup artifacts in upgrade state + add redaction for secrets
cli: progress reporter events + compact output + verbose details
tests: unit + integration coverage for failure handling and state persistence
docs: internal notes for flags and expected prerequisites
changed_areas: pkg/backup/snapshot/eks_ebs.go (EBS snapshot integration via CSI annotations/detection)
source.conversation_summary: Sean Gallagher: Can we make backup failure blocking by default? We should not mutate anything without a backup artifact recorded. Irene Choi: Yes—default is blocking; added explicit abort semantics and a hidden continue-on-failure flag with confirmation. Connor O'Brien: How does this behave for air-gapped/on-prem where S3 isn’t available? Irene Choi: Snapshot is the preferred path; logical backups can write to file:/// mounted paths. Added validation + remediation text if VolumeSnapshot CRDs are missing. Hanae Suzuki: Please confirm that state persistence doesn’t store credentials. Irene Choi: Backup destinations/credentials are read from existing secret refs; upgrade state stores only redacted URIs and snapshot IDs; added tests for redaction. Vanessa Ortiz: Snapshot selection needs to be explicit; we can’t snapshot everything by default. Irene Choi: Added allowlist labels + pvcSelector flag; default matches only control-plane DB + state PVC labels. Rafael Mendes: Add timeouts and surface duration in output so on-call can reason about stuck backups. Irene Choi: Added per-step timeout and duration metrics in progress events and state.
source.merge_outcome: Merged to main via squash after approvals; queued for inclusion in the next Private release train once ENG-4834 preflight framework lands in release branch.

source.title: Private upgrades: add upgrade plan generator (staged plan + artifact validation + auditable summary)
source.author: Devon Price
source.release_notes:
- Adds `redwood-private upgrade plan` to generate a staged, versioned upgrade plan artifact and summary.
- Validates required artifacts (charts/images/bundle manifest) before executing upgrades.
- Persists plan + inputs digest for auditability and support bundles (follow-up PR will wire support bundle ingestion).
commits: cmd: add `upgrade plan` subcommand + flags
planner: introduce stage/step model with stable IDs + schema version
artifacts: resolve sources (online registry vs local bundle) and validate availability
io: persist plan artifact + plan summary + input digests
tests: golden tests for plan generation + artifact validation unit tests
docs: help text + example output
source.body: Summary This PR introduces an upgrade plan generator to `redwood-private-installer` as the first building block for the Private upgrade + rollback mechanism. The generator produces a staged plan (control-plane first, then data-plane), validates required artifacts before any changes are made, and writes an auditable plan artifact + human-readable summary to disk. Motivation/Context We need a safe-by-default workflow for Redwood Private upgrades (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) with deterministic execution, clear failure modes, and artifacts that can be attached to support bundles and audit logs. The plan generator is intentionally separated from execution so operators (and Release Eng/SRE) can review what will happen before they run it. This aligns with ENG-4821 and the rollout UX direction in DES-311. What’s included - New CLI: `redwood-private upgrade plan` - Produces a versioned plan artifact (schema v1) with stable stage/step IDs. - Stages are ordered and explicit (preflight placeholder, artifact validation, backup placeholder, control-plane rollout placeholder, data-plane rollout placeholder, post-verify placeholder). Execution wiring will come in follow-up PRs; this PR focuses on plan generation and validation. - Artifact validation (fail-fast) - Validates the presence and resolvability of required artifacts for the target version: Helm chart references, required container images (by digest when available), and (when `--airgapped-bundle` is provided) bundle manifest presence and checksum file presence. Signature verification is intentionally out of scope here and will follow in PR-4871. - Produces actionable error messages including the specific missing artifact and the remediation hint (e.g., “configure --artifact-mirror” / “rebuild bundle” / “check registry connectivity”). - Auditable outputs - Writes the plan JSON and a summary text file under `${STATE_DIR}/upgrade-plans/<timestamp>-<from>-to-<to>/`. - Captures: planner version, schema version, CLI args (redacted), input digests (cluster ID, target version, artifact source), and a deterministic plan hash. - Summary includes a step table with stage IDs, blocking validations, and estimated operator prompts. Operator UX Example `redwood-private upgrade plan --from 1.18.2 --to 1.19.0 --artifact-source registry --state-dir /var/lib/redwood-private` Output (abridged): - Plan written: /var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade-plans/2025-04-24T18-22-10Z-1.18.2-to-1.19.0/plan.json - Summary written: /var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade-plans/2025-04-24T18-22-10Z-1.18.2-to-1.19.0/summary.txt - Plan hash: sha256:7b1c… Stages: - validate.artifacts (blocking) - backup.capture (blocking; placeholder until ENG-4838) - rollout.control_plane (blocking; placeholder until chart phase split lands) - rollout.data_plane (blocking; placeholder) - verify.post_upgrade (blocking; placeholder) Notes on determinism and safety - Stage and step IDs are stable across repeated runs for the same inputs; the only non-deterministic value is the output directory timestamp. The plan hash is computed over a canonical JSON form that excludes the timestamped path. - The plan generator does not attempt cluster mutations. It only reads config and validates artifact accessibility (network reachability checks are not included here; those will be part of preflight checks in PR-4834). Tests - Added unit tests for: - schema serialization and canonicalization - stable plan IDs across runs - artifact validation error surfaces - Golden plan fixtures for a handful of common configs (online registry, mirror, air-gapped bundle). Follow-ups (not in this PR) - Preflight check framework + checks (ENG-4829 / PR-4834) - Backup orchestration + snapshot hooks (ENG-4838 / PR-4850) - Rollback command and state machine (ENG-4844 / PR-4862) - Bundle signature verification + stricter offline validation (PR-4871) - Version compatibility gating integration (redwood PR-29104) Review notes - Please focus review on: plan schema shape, determinism, error messages/remediation hints, and state-dir file layout. - The plan schema is intentionally minimal and forward-compatible; we can extend with rollback hooks and migration markers once execution exists. Reviewer discussion highlights - Connor O’Brien: Requested schema version field and explicit “blocking” attribute per stage for change-management gating; addressed in commit “planner: schema_v1”. - Sean Gallagher: Asked for plan artifact redaction to avoid leaking registry creds/headers; CLI arg capture now redacts values for `--registry-password`, `--token`, and any env var expansion with `*_TOKEN`. - Kenji Nakamura: Requested summary output to be operator-first (step table + next actions) rather than raw JSON dump; summary formatter added and default output now includes a short table. - Sahana Iyer: Flagged a flaky golden test on Windows path separators in CI; normalized path rendering in tests and re-ran CI successfully. Merge outcome Squash-merged to main after approvals (Connor O’Brien, Sean Gallagher) and CI green. No runtime behavior changes until the execution path is wired; this is a new command and new internal planner package.

source.title: Implement pluggable preflight check framework for Private upgrades
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description: Summary: This PR introduces a pluggable preflight check framework in the Private installer. The framework standardizes check execution, result reporting, severity levels, and remediation pointers so upgrades are safer-by-default and failures are actionable for operators. Context: Part of the “Private upgrade + rollback mechanism” effort. This PR focuses on the framework + CLI surface for preflight runs; follow-up PRs will add/expand the actual checks (cluster health, storage, RBAC/SSO reachability, artifact availability, etc.). Goals: (1) Make prechecks composable (registerable plugins) and deterministic, (2) Provide consistent UX for operators and Support, (3) Establish blocking vs warning semantics that can be used by upgrade plan generation and change-management gating. Design notes: Checks are registered by ID and grouped into named phases (environment, control-plane, data-plane, artifacts). Each check returns a CheckResult with: status (PASS/WARN/FAIL/SKIP), severity (BLOCKER/WARNING/INFO), a short summary, optional details, and remediation pointers (human-readable steps + optional doc links). The runner supports timeouts per-check, a global deadline, and produces both console output and a JSON artifact suitable for audit/support bundles. CLI: Adds `redwood-private preflight run` (alias wired for upgrade flows in subsequent PRs). Supports `--format=table|json`, `--output <path>`, `--fail-on-warning` (default false), `--include`/`--exclude` (by check ID or group), and `--timeout` controls. Output example (json): `{ "run_id": "pf_...", "installer_version": "0.19.0", "target_version": "private-1.42.0", "results": [ ... ], "summary": { "blockers": 0, "warnings": 2 } }` (full schema in `docs/preflight-result-schema.md`). Implementation details: - New package `internal/preflight/` with: `registry` (check registration + metadata), `runner` (execution + concurrency limits), and `report` (renderers: table/json). - Standardized error taxonomy for checks to map common failures into remediation hints (e.g., kube auth failure vs API server unreachable vs RBAC forbidden). - Basic built-in checks included as scaffolding/examples: Kubernetes API reachability, namespace write access, and installer artifact writeability (output dir). These are intentionally minimal; real upgrade checks land in ENG-4829 follow-ups. - Adds a persistent “last preflight” record under the installer state dir to be consumed by the upgrade planner (plan generator PR) and support bundles. - Logging: all checks emit structured logs with `check_id`, `severity`, and `status` fields; runner emits a single summary line for easy grep during on-call. Operator UX: Table output uses stable ordering and explicitly labels “BLOCKING” items; remediation text is capped in the table view with a `--verbose` option to print full remediation steps. Testing: Unit tests cover registration, include/exclude filtering, timeout handling, renderer output stability, and JSON schema validation. Integration test spins up a kind cluster and validates kube reachability + RBAC check behavior under simulated restricted RBAC. Checklist: - [x] Framework + registry + runner - [x] JSON + table renderers - [x] Minimal built-in checks as examples - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] Docs: result schema + CLI usage - [ ] Add full precheck inventory (follow-up PRs under ENG-4829) Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) severity semantics (BLOCKER/WARNING/INFO) and defaults, (b) output stability (Support will consume this), and (c) security concerns around logging potentially sensitive info in remediation/details. Release notes: Adds new `preflight run` command and machine-readable preflight output artifact to support upgrade gating and troubleshooting.
commits: preflight: add check registry + metadata model
preflight: implement runner with timeouts and include/exclude filters
cli: add `preflight run` command with table/json output
report: add table renderer and JSON schema validation tests
tests: kind-based integration test for kube reachability + RBAC denial
docs: document preflight result schema and operator-facing CLI usage
review_conversation: Irene Choi: The framework looks good. One concern: remediation messages should never echo kubeconfig contents or full endpoint URLs for private clusters. Can we sanitize/trim? Bruno Silva: Good call. I updated the kube reachability check to log only hostnames (no tokens/paths) and redacted query strings; also added a `SafeDetails()` helper that defaults to redaction for error types we control.

Sean Gallagher: For gating, we need deterministic semantics: FAIL+BLOCKER must abort upgrade, WARN+BLOCKER should probably also abort by default. What’s the default behavior? Bruno Silva: Default is: any FAIL+BLOCKER returns non-zero exit code. WARN never fails unless `--fail-on-warning` is set. Severity is orthogonal; WARN+BLOCKER is allowed but we should avoid generating that combination in real checks. I added a validation in the registry to reject WARN+BLOCKER for now so we don’t create ambiguous gating behavior.

Kenji Nakamura: Table output truncation is helpful, but the operator needs a copy/paste remediation. Suggest printing remediation in full when there are blockers even without `--verbose`. Bruno Silva: Agreed. Updated renderer: if there is any blocking result, we append a “Remediation details” section with full steps for those checks. Non-blocking warnings remain truncated unless `--verbose`.

Marcus Lin: Please ensure we don’t allow check plugins to execute arbitrary shells by default. Are checks just Go functions in-process? Bruno Silva: Yes—checks are compiled into the installer binary and are registered at init time. No dynamic loading, no shell execution helpers added here. I also added a note in docs that checks must not run `kubectl`/shell and should use client-go to avoid leaking env state.

Sahana Iyer: Integration test flaked once on CI due to kind API server readiness. Can we add a wait/retry with bounded timeout? Bruno Silva: Added a readiness wait with exponential backoff (max 60s) before running the kube reachability check, and pinned kind node image to the version we use in other installer tests.

source.title: Wire up RBAC v2 admin bootstrap for Private installs (initial role bindings + IdP group mapping docs)
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.body:
Summary: This PR adds a safe, deterministic “admin bootstrap” path for RBAC v2 in Redwood Private installs to prevent lockout under deny-by-default. On first install/upgrade (when RBAC v2 is enabled), the installer will create an initial role binding granting a built-in admin role to a customer-specified subject (email or IdP group). This also documents the recommended approach for mapping IdP groups (SAML/OIDC) to Redwood roles in Private.

Context / Motivation: As we roll RBAC v2 into Private, new clusters can end up with RBAC enforcement enabled before any role bindings exist, resulting in a hard lockout where no identity has permissions to create the first role binding. Hosted/Dedicated can rely on internal provisioning workflows; Private needs an install-time “seed” that is explicit, auditable, and easy for customers to configure. This PR implements the minimum bootstrap mechanism (one initial binding + recovery tooling) and ties it to policy bundle versions so that it remains consistent across upgrades.

What’s included:
- Installer config/values to specify a bootstrap admin identity:
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.email` (single user email)
  - OR `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup` (IdP group string as emitted in claims)
  - Mutually exclusive; installer fails fast if both are set.
- Install/upgrade hook that (idempotently) creates:
  - RoleBinding: `bootstrap-admin` scoped to the org created by the installer (and cluster scope where required for installer-only operations)
  - Role: `org_admin` (reference to built-in template from the policy bundle; no ad-hoc permission list in installer)
  - Annotation: `rbac.redwood.ai/bootstrap=true` and `rbac.redwood.ai/policyBundleVersion=<version>` on the binding for evidence/debug.
- Explicit behavior gating:
  - No-op unless `RBAC_V2_ENABLED=true` (or Helm equivalent `rbacV2.enabled: true`).
  - If RBAC v2 is enabled but no bootstrap subject is provided, installer prints a blocking error with next steps (including recovery command) rather than proceeding.
- Recovery / break-fix command:
  - Adds `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin --email <user>` and `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin --idp-group <group>`.
  - Requires local cluster admin kubeconfig (i.e., this is a cluster-operator action) and emits an audit event `rbac.bootstrap_admin_binding.created`.
- Docs:
  - Adds a Private guide section: “Mapping IdP groups to Redwood RBAC roles” with examples for Okta (SAML), Azure AD (OIDC), and generic OIDC.
  - Documents recommended separation of duties: use a group-based binding for day-to-day admin, and keep a small break-glass user separate.

Implementation notes:
- Uses Control Plane RBAC v2 APIs when available; falls back to writing the RoleBinding CRD directly if the Control Plane is not yet reachable during install. Both paths converge on the same schema and labels.
- Idempotency: binding create is upsert-like. If the binding exists, installer verifies the subject and role match the requested config; if they differ, installer fails with a clear message to avoid silently escalating or breaking access.
- Scoping: bootstrap binding is created at org scope; additionally, for installer-required cluster-level operations (e.g., registering the cluster, health checks), a minimal `private_cluster_operator` role binding is created only when `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.includeClusterOps=true` is set. Default is false.
- Audit: for both installer and `rwctl` flows, we emit an audit log event containing subject type (user vs group), subject identifier, role template name, scope, and policy bundle version.

Config examples (Helm values):
- Email bootstrap:
  - `rbacV2.enabled: true`
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.email: "security-admin@customer.com"`
- Group bootstrap:
  - `rbacV2.enabled: true`
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup: "Redwood-Org-Admins"`

Docs excerpt: For OIDC, ensure your IdP includes groups in the ID token or userinfo response and that the claim name configured in Redwood matches. Example: `oidc.groupClaim: "groups"` and then bind `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup` to a string present in that claim.

Testing:
- Added an integration test that brings up a minimal Private control plane with RBAC v2 enabled and asserts:
  - Bootstrap RoleBinding is created.
  - A user with matching email can call `/v1/orgs/{id}/roles:list` (sanity check).
  - A non-matching identity receives `PERMISSION_DENIED`.
- Added unit tests for config validation, idempotent upsert behavior, and Control Plane unavailable fallback path.

Operational considerations:
- Upgrade path: If upgrading an existing cluster to RBAC v2, bootstrap binding is only created if no admin bindings exist (to avoid changing existing customer setups). Installer logs whether it created or skipped bootstrap.
- Evidence: The binding annotations and audit event are intended to be attachable in SOC2 evidence packets for “authorized administrators provisioned via documented process”.

Related:
- Linear: ENG-18410 (Private installer admin bootstrap), ENG-18388 (feature-flag rollout)
- Follow-up (not included): Add Console copy linking to Private docs for group claim troubleshooting; tracked in DES-927.

Checklist:
- [x] Idempotent bootstrap binding creation
- [x] Fast-fail on misconfiguration (both email and group)
- [x] Audit event emitted
- [x] Docs for IdP group mapping (SAML/OIDC)
- [x] Integration tests for fresh install
commits: installer: validate rbacV2 bootstrap config + surface actionable errors
installer: create bootstrap RoleBinding on install/upgrade (idempotent)
rwctl: add rbac bootstrap-admin recovery command
docs(private): document IdP group mapping to roles + examples
tests: add private install RBAC v2 bootstrap integration test
review_conversation: Marcus Lin: Please ensure we never proceed with RBAC v2 enabled and no bootstrap binding (deny-by-default lockout). Also need an auditable marker on the bootstrap binding and an audit event for evidence.

Benji Okafor: Agreed. Added blocking validation + annotations `rbac.redwood.ai/bootstrap=true` and policy bundle version, and emitted `rbac.bootstrap_admin_binding.created` from both installer and rwctl paths. Pushed 2 more commits.
Kaitlyn Nguyen: For OIDC group mapping, customers will be confused about claim names vs group values. Can we add a doc example showing the `groupClaim` setting and what group strings look like?
Benji Okafor: Added OIDC section with Okta + Azure AD examples and explicit note about claim name matching, plus troubleshooting commands to inspect token claims.
Ines Carvalho: Scope question: Do we need cluster-scope permissions for the bootstrap user? I’d prefer least privilege by default.
Benji Okafor: Default remains org-admin only; cluster ops binding is opt-in via `includeClusterOps`. Installer logs which scopes were created.
Vivek Kulkarni: For pilot customers, we should recommend group-based bootstrap (not a single person). Also call out a break-glass user separately.

Benji Okafor: Added docs guidance: primary admin via group binding + separate break-glass user. Also clarified that bootstrap binding can be rotated by creating a new binding and deleting the bootstrap one.
release_notes: Private installs with RBAC v2 enabled now require specifying a bootstrap admin (email or IdP group) to prevent lockout.
Added `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin` recovery command and docs for IdP group-to-role mapping.

title: Wire up RBAC v2 admin bootstrap for Private installs (initial role bindings + IdP group mapping docs)
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a safe, deterministic “admin bootstrap” path for RBAC v2 in Redwood Private installs to prevent lockout under deny-by-default. On first install/upgrade (when RBAC v2 is enabled), the installer will create an initial role binding granting a built-in admin role to a customer-specified subject (email or IdP group). This also documents the recommended approach for mapping IdP groups (SAML/OIDC) to Redwood roles in Private.

Context / Motivation: As we roll RBAC v2 into Private, new clusters can end up with RBAC enforcement enabled before any role bindings exist, resulting in a hard lockout where no identity has permissions to create the first role binding. Hosted/Dedicated can rely on internal provisioning workflows; Private needs an install-time “seed” that is explicit, auditable, and easy for customers to configure. This PR implements the minimum bootstrap mechanism (one initial binding + recovery tooling) and ties it to policy bundle versions so that it remains consistent across upgrades.

What’s included:
- Installer config/values to specify a bootstrap admin identity:
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.email` (single user email)
  - OR `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup` (IdP group string as emitted in claims)
  - Mutually exclusive; installer fails fast if both are set.
- Install/upgrade hook that (idempotently) creates:
  - RoleBinding: `bootstrap-admin` scoped to the org created by the installer (and cluster scope where required for installer-only operations)
  - Role: `org_admin` (reference to built-in template from the policy bundle; no ad-hoc permission list in installer)
  - Annotation: `rbac.redwood.ai/bootstrap=true` and `rbac.redwood.ai/policyBundleVersion=<version>` on the binding for evidence/debug.
- Explicit behavior gating:
  - No-op unless `RBAC_V2_ENABLED=true` (or Helm equivalent `rbacV2.enabled: true`).
  - If RBAC v2 is enabled but no bootstrap subject is provided, installer prints a blocking error with next steps (including recovery command) rather than proceeding.
- Recovery / break-fix command:
  - Adds `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin --email <user>` and `rwctl rbac bootstrap-admin --idp-group <group>`.
  - Requires local cluster admin kubeconfig (i.e., this is a cluster-operator action) and emits an audit event `rbac.bootstrap_admin_binding.created`.
- Docs:
  - Adds a Private guide section: “Mapping IdP groups to Redwood RBAC roles” with examples for Okta (SAML), Azure AD (OIDC), and generic OIDC.
  - Documents recommended separation of duties: use a group-based binding for day-to-day admin, and keep a small break-glass user separate.

Implementation notes:
- Uses Control Plane RBAC v2 APIs when available; falls back to writing the RoleBinding CRD directly if the Control Plane is not yet reachable during install. Both paths converge on the same schema and labels.
- Idempotency: binding create is upsert-like. If the binding exists, installer verifies the subject and role match the requested config; if they differ, installer fails with a clear message to avoid silently escalating or breaking access.
- Scoping: bootstrap binding is created at org scope; additionally, for installer-required cluster-level operations (e.g., registering the cluster, health checks), a minimal `private_cluster_operator` role binding is created only when `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.includeClusterOps=true` is set. Default is false.
- Audit: for both installer and `rwctl` flows, we emit an audit log event containing subject type (user vs group), subject identifier, role template name, scope, and policy bundle version.

Config examples (Helm values):
- Email bootstrap:
  - `rbacV2.enabled: true`
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.email: "security-admin@customer.com"`
- Group bootstrap:
  - `rbacV2.enabled: true`
  - `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup: "Redwood-Org-Admins"`

Docs excerpt: For OIDC, ensure your IdP includes groups in the ID token or userinfo response and that the claim name configured in Redwood matches. Example: `oidc.groupClaim: "groups"` and then bind `rbacV2.bootstrapAdmin.idpGroup` to a string present in that claim.

Testing:
- Added an integration test that brings up a minimal Private control plane with RBAC v2 enabled and asserts:
  - Bootstrap RoleBinding is created.
  - A user with matching email can call `/v1/orgs/{id}/roles:list` (sanity check).
  - A non-matching identity receives `PERMISSION_DENIED`.
- Added unit tests for config validation, idempotent upsert behavior, and Control Plane unavailable fallback path.

Operational considerations:
- Upgrade path: If upgrading an existing cluster to RBAC v2, bootstrap binding is only created if no admin bindings exist (to avoid changing existing customer setups). Installer logs whether it created or skipped bootstrap.
- Evidence: The binding annotations and audit event are intended to be attachable in SOC2 evidence packets for “authorized administrators provisioned via documented process”.

Related:
- Linear: ENG-18410 (Private installer admin bootstrap), ENG-18388 (feature-flag rollout)
- Follow-up (not included): Add Console copy linking to Private docs for group claim troubleshooting; tracked in DES-927.

Checklist:
- [x] Idempotent bootstrap binding creation
- [x] Fast-fail on misconfiguration (both email and group)
- [x] Audit event emitted
- [x] Docs for IdP group mapping (SAML/OIDC)
- [x] Integration tests for fresh install

source.title: Private installer: pinning requirements for Runtime LTS artifacts (checksums, digests, rollback)
source.author: Bruno Silva
source.description:
Context: This PR is primarily a requirements/alignment thread captured in the PR description + review comments to unblock implementation of Runtime LTS pinning in the Redwood Private installer. Dedicated/Private enterprise customers want: (1) the ability to pin a specific runtime patch (e.g., 1.18.1-lts) for 6–12+ months, (2) verifiable artifacts (checksums/signatures/provenance) that can be audited, and (3) a predictable rollback path when a patch introduces regressions. This PR documents and finalizes the installer-facing requirements so Release Eng can publish the right metadata and Runtime can keep semantics stable across the LTS line.

Requirements agreed in this thread:
- Pin semantics (must be explicit):
  - Installer MUST support pinning to an exact LTS patch version string (e.g., runtime=1.18.1-lts) and MUST NOT silently upgrade within the LTS line.
  - Installer SHOULD optionally support pinning by immutable image digest (sha256:...) to satisfy strict change control.
  - If both version and digest are provided, digest wins and the installer records both for audit.
- Artifact verification (enterprise default):
  - Installer MUST download a release manifest for the selected patch containing: image tag, image digest, per-arch digests, sha256 checksums for auxiliary tarballs, provenance pointer, and release notes URL.
  - Installer MUST verify sha256 checksums of downloaded tarballs (air-gapped flow) and MUST verify the runtime image digest matches the manifest when pulling from registry.
  - Signature verification: in this phase we capture requirement + interface; implementation may be gated behind availability of signing keys/verification tooling (tracked in ENG-4832). The installer will accept a --verify-signatures flag (default on when signatures exist; fallback to checksum-only with a loud warning).
- Rollback (operator safe):
  - Installer MUST support rollback to the previously installed runtime patch (one-step rollback) and MUST preserve the previous runtime image reference (tag + digest) and manifest in state.
  - Rollback MUST be supported for both online registry pulls and air-gapped artifact bundles.
  - Rollback MUST not require re-running full cluster bootstrap; it should be a targeted runtime component rollback.
- Observability + audit:
  - Installer MUST write an install record capturing: selected channel, requested version, resolved version, resolved digests, manifest checksum, and timestamps.
  - Installer MUST surface a single command to print the current pinned version + digests for audit (used in SOC2/ISO evidence requests).
- UX + defaults:
  - Default behavior for Private is unchanged (track recommended stable), but when customers opt into LTS they should see an explicit prompt/flag path that makes the “no auto-upgrades” property clear.
  - For regulated customers, we expect most to use exact version pinning, with digest pinning as a stricter option.

Non-requirements explicitly called out:
- Not promising that Private installer can automatically select “the newest patch in the LTS line” (that conflicts with change control expectations).
- Not implementing full signature verification unless Release Eng artifacts are signed consistently; however, we are locking the installer interface now.

Implementation notes included here to keep scope tight:
- New config knobs will be added under runtime: channel (stable|lts), version, digest, verify_checksums, verify_signatures.
- Installer will fetch a small JSON manifest published alongside LTS releases (owned by Release Eng). For air-gapped, customers can supply the manifest file path.
- We record state in the existing installer state directory and store the previous runtime reference for rollback.

Why a PR for requirements: This was the fastest way to converge across Release Eng, Security, and Private packaging while we already had PR-663 open for the pinning feature work. The conversation below is the canonical alignment and is referenced in the follow-up implementation commits.
commits: docs(installer): add LTS pinning requirements to PR body for cross-team review
installer: add runtime pin config fields (version/digest/verify flags) and state recording
installer: implement manifest fetch + sha256 verification for air-gapped bundles
installer: persist previous runtime ref and add rollback command scaffolding
tests: add unit tests for resolve logic (version vs digest precedence) and manifest validation
source.conversation:
Thread: clarifying requirements for Private installer pinning, checksums, and rollback for LTS artifacts

[2025-02-24] Bruno Silva (author): Pulling the requirements conversation into this PR so we can lock semantics before I land more code. The key question: do we pin by tag (1.18.1-lts) or digest, and what verification is mandatory for regulated Private?

[2025-02-24] Vivek Kulkarni: For regulated Private, tag pinning is necessary but not sufficient. Procurement wants a change ticket that points to immutable artifacts. We should support digest pinning (optional) but must at least record resolved digest even when user pins by tag. Also: no “auto-latest patch” behavior; customers explicitly approve upgrades.

[2025-02-24] Connor O'Brien: +1. Release Eng will publish an LTS manifest per patch. Proposal: installer takes runtime_version=1.18.1-lts, resolves to a manifest that includes runtime image digest(s) and checksums. If digest is supplied, skip resolution and just verify digest matches manifest when available. We’ll wire the manifest format into the LTS release pipeline.

[2025-02-25] Sahana Iyer: From CI/build standpoint, checksum verification is the minimum bar. Signature verification depends on getting key management + verification tooling into the installer runtime. Let’s add the flags now and make verify-signatures a no-op with a warning until artifacts are signed end-to-end.

[2025-02-25] Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please make the failure modes explicit: (1) missing manifest should be a hard error when channel=lts unless --allow-unverified is set; (2) checksum mismatch must hard fail; (3) signature missing should warn (for now) but be designed to become a hard fail later. We need an audit record for compliance.

[2025-02-26] Sean Gallagher: Rollback is critical. We need a one-step rollback that Support can walk customers through at 2am. Keep it targeted to runtime components and don’t touch unrelated services. Also include a “print current pinned version+digest” command so SRE can quickly confirm what’s running.

[2025-02-26] Bruno Silva: Updated the plan accordingly: explicit version pin, optional digest pin, always record resolved digest, manifest required for LTS, checksum verification hard fail. Added design for rollback to previous runtime ref.

[2025-02-27] Connor O'Brien: Confirmed: manifest will include release notes URL and SBOM pointer (if present). For first LTS patches we may have SBOM stub; that’s OK as long as we’re explicit.

[2025-02-28] Vivek Kulkarni: One more requirement: the installer should not change the pinned version on reruns unless the operator explicitly changes config. No surprise drift.

[2025-03-01] Bruno Silva: Implemented. State file stores requested + resolved refs; rerun compares and is a no-op unless config changes.

[2025-03-03] Dr. Aisha Rahman: Approved with comment: please ensure the audit record includes manifest checksum and timestamp; that’s needed for audit trails.

[2025-03-03] Connor O'Brien: Approved. We’ll align the manifest format with ENG-4832 and update Release Eng docs accordingly.

[2025-03-03] Vivek Kulkarni: Approved. This matches customer expectations from SUP-18506.
release_notes: Private installer can pin Redwood Runtime to an exact LTS patch version and record resolved digests for audit.
Installer verifies checksums/digests against a release manifest and supports one-step rollback to the previously installed runtime patch.
Added operator commands to print current pinned runtime version/digest and installation audit metadata.

source.title: Wire retention defaults into Private install + document purge job backing services
source.author: Vivek Kulkarni
source.release_notes:
- Private installs now ship with secure retention defaults aligned to v1 retention controls tiers (audit logs + request logs TTLs, verification metrics enabled).
- Installer docs now explicitly list required backing services (DB/index/object storage/cron runner) for purge jobs and verification.
source.description:
Title: Wire retention defaults into Private install + document purge job backing services

Context
Private customers (regulated workloads) are asking two recurring questions during security reviews: (1) what are the default retention behaviors when they install Redwood Private, and (2) what infrastructure is required to reliably enforce purges (scheduled jobs, storage/indexing dependencies, and verification signals). This PR updates the private installer to ship private-friendly defaults for retention + audit logging and adds a dedicated doc section describing purge job prerequisites.

Scope
- Set secure retention defaults in the installer Helm values (aligned to retention controls v1): conservative request/response log retention, longer audit log retention, and explicit “disabled logging” behavior where applicable.
- Enable purge job runner + recommended throttles by default (idempotent, bounded deletes) with opt-out in values.
- Document required backing services for purge enforcement (what is mandatory vs recommended) and what to validate post-install (dashboards/metrics).
- Document upgrade notes (how to migrate from older installs that lack retention sections in values.yaml).

Non-goals
- This does not implement TTL enforcement itself (handled in redwood monorepo + telemetry stores).
- This does not add legal hold UI/flows (control plane feature).

Key decisions
- Defaults are “secure by default” for Private: request/response logs are short-lived unless explicitly extended; audit logs default to longer retention to satisfy auditability expectations.
- Purge jobs are enabled by default but throttled to avoid DB pressure. Operators can tune batch size / max runtime.
- Installer docs treat purge backing services as first-class dependencies: if you don’t deploy them, retention will degrade (policy is accepted but enforcement can lag/fail).

Defaults (Private)
- audit_logs: enabled, default TTL 365d (configurable)
- request_response_logs: enabled, default TTL 30d (configurable; can be set to 0 to disable storage)
- traces: default TTL 14d (if trace storage enabled)
- metrics: default TTL 30d (if long-term metrics storage enabled)
- artifacts (eval datasets/report packages): default TTL 90d when stored in object storage (if compliance pack enabled)

Implementation notes
- Adds a `retention:` section to the installer chart values with explicit per-class TTLs and a single place to switch purge jobs on/off.
- Adds `purgeJobs:` tuning (schedule, concurrency, maxRowsPerRun, maxRuntimeSeconds) with safe defaults.
- Adds docs: “Retention & Purge Enforcement Requirements” (DB/index/object storage, job runner, permissions, monitoring)

Validation
- Install on kind + minikube profiles: chart renders with and without optional stores.
- Values schema validation (helm lint + kubeconform) updated for new fields.
- Smoke test: retention config is visible in control plane and purge job CronJobs are created when enabled.

Rollout
- Recommended to land before enabling retention controls by default in Private distributions. Backward compatible (existing values files remain valid; new defaults apply when fields absent).
commits: installer(chart): add retention defaults + purge job toggles
installer(values): introduce retention.* per data class TTLs
docs(private): document purge backing services + post-install verification
ci: extend helm schema checks for retention section
chore: upgrade note for pre-retention installs
changed_files: scripts/render-helm.sh (ensure schema validation runs in CI)
conversation:
Priya Natarajan (review): Can you cite where the default TTL numbers are coming from (PRD/overview) and ensure they match what Security wants to message externally?

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Added a short “Source of truth” note to the doc linking to PM-842 and the retention controls overview; also aligned request_response_logs default to 30d (was 60d) to match the current Security tier defaults for Private.
Marcus Lin (review): Two concerns: (1) enabling purge jobs by default could surprise operators if backing services aren’t installed; (2) we need to be explicit that retention policy acceptance != enforcement if stores are missing.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Updated docs with a callout: “Retention config is accepted, but enforcement depends on backing services.” Also made purge jobs default-enabled but added a startup log + Helm NOTES that warns when purgeJobs.enabled=true but optional stores are not configured.
Sean Gallagher (review): Please keep default concurrency low and provide a max runtime. We saw backlog after maintenance (similar to incidents thread). Also add guidance on what metrics to watch to confirm purge is healthy.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Set default `purgeJobs.concurrency=1`, `maxRowsPerRun=50_000`, `maxRuntimeSeconds=900`. Added a verification section listing ttl_lag_seconds, purge_failures_total, purge_rows_deleted_total and recommended alert thresholds.
Allison Grant (review): Can we avoid introducing high-cardinality metrics labels from the installer side? Also ensure docs point to dashboards rather than telling operators to build their own.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): No metric label changes in this repo; only toggles. Docs now reference the standard “Retention Controls Verification” dashboards (observability pack) and how to import them.
Kevin Osei (review): For compliance pack customers, we should mention object storage requirement (bucket lifecycle vs application TTL) and that report packages may include retention config export.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Added a subsection for “Compliance reporting pack artifacts” describing object storage requirement, recommended bucket lifecycle policies, and note that exports can be bundled with audit log exports.
CI note: kubeconform failed on first run due to missing schema update for new `retention.artifacts.ttlDays` field. Fixed in follow-up commit; reran workflow.
Priya Natarajan (approval): Looks good now. Please ensure we add this to the next private installer release notes.

Vivek Kulkarni (author): Added release_notes field + updated docs/operations/upgrade-notes.md.

source.title: Private installer: add backup format versioning, restore compatibility checks, and migration/operator messaging
source.author: Michael Nguyen
source.release_notes: Private: backups now include an explicit format version and restore performs compatibility checks with actionable operator messages. Adds an escape hatch to proceed with unsupported restores (explicit flag) and documents migration expectations.
source.description:
Context: We’ve had a few confusing restore failures in staging and in at least one on-prem customer scenario (SUP-18477) where the operator did not realize the backup artifact was produced by an older installer/runtime and required either (a) a restore-time migration step, or (b) a compatible installer version. This PR introduces explicit backup format versioning and a restore-time compatibility gate, plus clearer operator-facing errors and a short “migration notes” doc to reduce Support/SRE escalation time.

What this PR does (high level): 1) Adds a required `format_version` field to the backup manifest and embeds it into the artifact metadata, 2) Adds compatibility checks early in `restore apply` (before doing any destructive actions), including minimum/maximum supported versions and a readable “why” message, 3) Introduces explicit error codes and remediation hints (upgrade/downgrade installer, run a migration helper, verify CRDs/Helm charts), 4) Adds `--allow-unsupported-backup` escape hatch with loud warnings + telemetry marker, 5) Adds MIGRATIONS.md documenting known format deltas and how to handle them.

Backup format changes: - Backup manifest now contains: `format_version`, `created_by.installer_version`, `created_by.git_sha`, `created_at`, and `components[]` entries with per-component schema versions. - Default `format_version` is `2` for new backups. - Restore still supports `format_version=1` (from PR-482 era artifacts) with read-only “compatibility translation” for a small subset of fields.

Compatibility policy (as implemented): - Installer supports restoring backups with `format_version` in `[min_supported, max_supported]` defined in `internal/backup/compat/compat.go`. - If `format_version` is below `min_supported`, restore exits with `RWBI-RESTORE-0007` and guidance to use an older installer OR run an offline migration tool (if available). - If `format_version` is above `max_supported`, restore exits with `RWBI-RESTORE-0008` and guidance to upgrade the installer bundle to a newer release. - For on-prem/air-gapped, the message explicitly calls out “use the installer bundle shipped with the same release train as the backup” and points to `docs/MIGRATIONS.md` shipped in the bundle.

Operator-facing errors (examples): - RBAC/CRD ordering errors now include detected missing kinds and a suggested command to verify: `kubectl get crd | grep redwood` (best-effort). - S3/NFS backend errors include the resolved backend type and a pointer to the config key path that was missing/invalid. - KMS envelope encryption misconfig includes a hint to confirm KMS reachability and key policy (but does not print key IDs or sensitive material).

CLI changes: - `backup create` now writes `format_version` to the manifest and prints a one-line summary: `Backup created: format=v2 installer=0.18.3 artifact=...` - `restore apply` now performs compatibility checks immediately after reading and verifying the manifest checksum (before acquiring cluster locks). - New flags: `restore apply --allow-unsupported-backup` (defaults false), `restore apply --print-compat` (prints min/max supported and exits 0).

Security considerations: - No change to the encryption primitives in this PR; this only adds metadata + checks. - The escape hatch flag is intentionally noisy and logs an event marker for auditability. - Error messages avoid leaking secrets/paths beyond what’s required for remediation.

Testing: - Unit tests for compatibility matrix + manifest parsing. - Integration test adds a `fixtures/backup_v1/` artifact to ensure v1 restores are still accepted and surfaced as “legacy” in logs. - Manual: restored v1 and v2 backups in staging; verified we fail fast on an artificially bumped format_version.

Rollout notes: - New backups created after this merge are v2. - Restores remain backward compatible to v1; we should keep v1 support until we’re past the current customer upgrade window.

Related: - PR-482 (backup create), PR-489 (restore apply workflow + validation), PR-497 (air-gapped import/export).
commits: backup(manifest): add format_version and created_by metadata
restore: add compatibility gate and structured error codes
compat: define min/max supported versions + print-compat output
docs: add MIGRATIONS.md and update restore help text
tests: add v1 fixture + compatibility unit tests
chore: tighten log redaction for restore error paths
review_conversation: Ethan Park: Can we ensure the compatibility check runs before any namespace/CRD mutations? We’ve had ‘half-applied’ restores in the past.

Michael Nguyen: Yes — moved the compat gate to immediately after manifest verification and before acquiring the restore lock / applying resources. Added a unit test to assert ordering in restore pipeline.

Connor O'Brien: For air-gapped, the error message should explicitly point to docs shipped in the bundle, not internal Confluence. Also, do we want to fail if format_version is missing (old artifacts)?

Michael Nguyen: Updated messaging to reference docs/MIGRATIONS.md and docs/backup_restore_compatibility.md (both included in bundle). For missing format_version, we treat as v1 with a deprecation warning; added RWBI-RESTORE-0012 warning log.
Hanae Suzuki: Please confirm we’re not printing KMS key identifiers or ciphertext blobs in error output. Also, the escape hatch needs an audit marker.

Michael Nguyen: Confirmed redaction; added tests around error rendering. Escape hatch now logs `restore.compat_override=true` and prints a warning block requiring operator confirmation (y/N prompt unless --yes).
Sean Gallagher: Suggest adding an error code mapping doc so on-call can search quickly. Also, can `--print-compat` be used in smoke tests?
Michael Nguyen: Added a short table to docs/backup_restore_compatibility.md and wired `--print-compat` for SRE automation; it exits 0 and prints min/max + installer version.
Fatima Noor: On-prem restore often fails due to missing CRDs/ordering (SUP-18477). Can we detect missing CRDs earlier and give a better message?
Michael Nguyen: Added best-effort CRD presence check in preflight (non-fatal for some kinds, fatal for required ones) and includes suggested commands. Linked SUP-18477 in description.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Connor O'Brien
post_merge_followups: Open: Align docs site copy with MIGRATIONS.md wording (tracked in ENG-9828)
Open: Decide deprecation timeline for implicit v1 backups without format_version (propose: warn in 0.19.x, hard fail in 0.22.x) (tracked in ENG-9830)

source.title: Introduce DNS-forwarder shadowing and deterministic SG naming for private control plane
source.author: Sana Iyer
source.description: Motivation: Private control plane rollouts sometimes experience DNS transient failure during cross-account peering and SG rotation. This PR introduces a lightweight, opt-in "shadowed" DNS forwarder that mirrors control plane DNS zones into a customer-managed private hosted zone and a deterministic security-group naming scheme to make policy and rotation idempotent across re-applies. What changed: Terraform module additions (control-plane/dns-forwarder, network/sg-naming); Helm chart values to enable the forwarder as a DaemonSet for on-prem installs; an upgrade job that creates provisional shadow zones and validates DNS propagation; a small operator-side hook for safe SG swaps during migration. Rollout plan: Default behavior is unchanged. Operators can enable the feature via the private-installer helm values or by setting enable_shadow_dns = true in the Terraform module. Rollout steps are documented in upgrade_notes.md and include preflight checks, staged canary in a single AZ, and automated rollback if propagation or endpoint auth fails. Backwards compatibility: The DNS forwarder is additive; when disabled the installer behaves identically to previous releases. The SG naming scheme will not change existing SGs unless the migration job is explicitly run; migration is reversible. Security considerations: forwarder binds to VPC endpoints only (ENIs are ephemeral) and uses cross-account endpoint authorization tokens; IAM policy changes are scoped and documented. Related work: ties into ENG-7221 (dns resiliency) and ENG-7304 (safer sg rotation).
source.commits:
1) add: terraform module control-plane/dns-forwarder (init, IAM, route53-conditional)
2) add: network/sg-naming module and migration script
3) update: helm/controlplane values + daemonset template for dns-forwarder
4) add: upgrade job and preflight checks (scripts/upgrade/shadow-dns-validate.sh)
5) docs: upgrade_notes.md and operational playbook
6) tests: unit + integration (mock route53, peering auth)
review_comments: Liam O'Connor: Overall looks good. Two asks: ensure migration path does not cause SG churn during autoscaling events, and add more logging to the forwarder for DNS resolution failures.
Sana Iyer: Addressed both — added stable-name label to SGs and rate-limited logging with structured events. See new commits that update sg-naming and daemonset args.
Wei Zhang: Terraform module should avoid implicit depends_on where possible; convert some resources to use 'for_each' and explicit lifecycle prevent_destroy where appropriate.
Sana Iyer: Reworked module to for_each the endpoint authorizations and added lifecycle blocks for the transition resources. Also added an opt-out var to skip migration jobs.
Priya Natarajan: One concern: cross-account endpoint tokens must be rotated safely. Recommend adding a short-lived token mechanism and documenting rotation steps.
Sana Iyer: Added token rotation notes in docs and a helper script in scripts/rotate_endpoint_token.sh; token TTL default is 15m and configurable.
Liam O'Connor: CI showed a flaky network test — reran and passed. Approving for squash+merge.
Wei Zhang: LGTM (approved).
MergeBot: Merged via squash with commit message: 'feat(installer): add shadowed DNS forwarder + deterministic SG naming and migration job (ENG-7221, ENG-7304)'.
source.release_notes: Added optional shadowed DNS forwarder for private control plane deployments to improve cross-account DNS resiliency; introduced deterministic SG naming and a reversible migration job to safely rename/manage security groups. Opt-in via Terraform/Helm. See docs/upgrade_notes.md for rollout steps.

source.title: offline-bootstrap: OCI index + terraform mirror module for air-gapped registry bootstrapping
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: customers deploying Redwood Private in fully air-gapped datacenters requested a more deterministic bootstrap flow for registries and OS images. Current airgap bundles are large and monolithic which makes incremental updates and signature verification awkward during upgrades. This PR introduces a compact OCI image index format for runtime bundles, and a Terraform module to provision a local mirror + registry-signing step in offline environments. Changes include: 1) a new 'oci-index' manifest generator that emits per-arch component descriptors (runtime images, charts, kernel modules) and an LRU-aware delta manifest pointer for efficient staged upgrades; 2) a Terraform module redwood-terraform/registry-mirror which wires up a local registry, preloads index manifests, and produces a signed admission config used by the installer; 3) Helm chart hooks to verify image signatures using cosign during pre-install, and a lightweight pre-pull controller that validates manifests against the pinned index; 4) installer upgrade/rollback flow updates: atomic manifest pinning with symlinked index bundles and a fast rollback hook that swaps pointer files and triggers a controlled reconcile. This description includes implementation notes, test plan, and rollout strategy. Implementation notes: the oci-index generator is written in Go and produces index.json alongside OCI blobs; each entry includes a semantic component tag (eg runtime:v1.42.0) and a digest pin. The Terraform module supports grabbing index artifacts from an offline bundle, uploading to a local registry, and producing a signed keypair bundle (KMS compatible) for the installer to verify at runtime. The Helm hook runs as a pre-install Job that verifies signatures and pre-pulls images into node-local cache. Testing: unit tests added for index generator, integration tests for the Terraform module using localbin (kind + registry) in CI, and an optional e2e nightly airgapped pipeline that exercises upgrade/rollback. Rollout: feature gated behind installer flag --offline-oci-index and disabled by default; will be exposed to customers as experimental for two releases. Checklist: - [x] OCI index generator (Go) - [x] Terraform registry-mirror module - [x] Helm pre-install signature verification hook - [x] Installer manifest pin/rollback hooks - [x] Unit/integration tests - [x] Docs and upgrade notes Backwards compatibility: existing monolithic bundles are still supported by the installer; when --offline-oci-index is not enabled, bootstrapping behaves exactly as before. Security: cosign is used to verify images; private key material is expected to live in customer KMS and is never uploaded to Redwood systems. Related: Linear ENG-3942 (airgap registry UX), ENG-4037 (upgrade safety).
source.commits:
1) Add oci-index generator and unit tests (A. Chen)
2) Add terraform/registry-mirror module and docs (A. Chen)
3) Add helm pre-install cosign hook and pre-pull controller (D. Martinez)
4) Installer: atomic manifest pointer + rollback strategy (R. Singh)
5) CI: add integration job for registry-mirror (CI Bot)
source.release_notes: Introduce experimental OCI index based offline bundles and a Terraform registry-mirror module to streamline air-gapped bootstrapping and staged upgrades. Disabled by default behind --offline-oci-index flag.
source.review_comments:
Diego Martinez: Nice work on the index format — can we include a small TTL field per component to help garbage collection?
Ava Chen: Added ttl_seconds to descriptors and documented GC behavior in docs/airgap/oci-index.md.
Riya Patel: CI flaked on the integration job the first run (registry timing). Added retries and a wait-for-registry helper to the test harness.
Owen Walker: Suggest adding a document describing key rotation for signed indexes.
Ava Chen: Added a key rotation subsection and example scripts.
Reviewers approved after two iterations.
source.notes_on_testing:
Unit tests: ./cmd/oci-index tests cover manifest generation and digest pinning.
Integration: terraform apply against local registry (kind) in CI; cosign verification end-to-end in an ephemeral cluster.
E2E: nightly pipeline (optional) performs full bootstrap from oci-index bundle and simulates upgrade + rollback.
source.upgrade_plan: Ship as experimental in v1.17. Customers opt-in via installer flag. First release will include migration docs for maintainers who want to convert monolithic bundles to OCI index bundles using provided tooling. After stable signal (2 releases, positive customer feedback) flip default to enabled and deprecate monolithic path with a 6-month warning.
source.security_considerations: Private keys are KMS-managed and only referenced during offline signing; cosign public keys are bundled with the index but validator code verifies trust chain. Installer refuses to boot when signature verification fails. All network touches are local to the customer's network during bootstrap.
source.observability_changes: Added metrics: installer.offline.index_verification_duration_ms, installer.offline.prepull_images_count, registry_mirror.upload_size_bytes. Dashboards updated in docs/dashboards/offline-index.md.
source.notes: This is intentionally conservative in behavioral changes: existing customers are unaffected unless they opt-in. The design aims to reduce bundle churn and to allow small delta updates in highly regulated offline environments.

source.title: Add `restore apply` workflow with preflight checks and post-restore validation report
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.release_notes: - Private installer: introduce `restore apply` with deterministic ordering, guardrails, and a support-ready validation report. - Adds optional `--validate-only` mode and clearer failure categorization (preflight/artifact/restore/validation).
source.description: Implements the end-to-end restore path for Redwood Private in the installer CLI. This adds a new `restore apply` command that: (1) performs strict preflight checks (cluster connectivity, namespace/CRD readiness, version compatibility, storage backend access, and integrity checks on the backup artifact), (2) restores control-plane state in a deterministic order (cluster prerequisites, control-plane chart values/config, DB restore, then service rollout), and (3) runs post-restore health checks and emits a validation report that Support/SRE can use for escalation. The goal is to make restores repeatable, safer to run under time pressure, and easier to debug when something goes wrong. Restore ordering is intentionally conservative to avoid the on-prem issues we saw in SUP-18477 (missing CRDs/incorrect bootstrap order). Security note: we do not restore secret material from backups; we restore secret references and validate that required KMS/external secret providers are reachable. The report includes sanitized environment details (versions, timestamps, artifact IDs), step-by-step results, and pointers to relevant logs. Usage examples: `private-installer restore apply --artifact s3://<bucket>/backups/<id> --namespace redwood-private --report /tmp/restore-report.json` and `private-installer restore apply --artifact ./backup.tgz.enc --offline --validate-only`.
commits: cli: add `restore apply` subcommand and shared step runner
restore: add preflight checks (cluster, RBAC, CRDs, versions, storage, artifact)
restore: implement deterministic bootstrap ordering + rollback/abort semantics
validation: add post-restore probes and report emitter
docs: update installer help text + examples for S3/NFS/offline restores
changed_files: internal/restore/artifact/*.go (manifest parsing, checksum verification, decrypt hooks)
internal/restore/validation/*.go (health probes, endpoint checks, routing/config assertions)
internal/logging/redaction.go (redact tokens, keys, and customer identifiers)
ci_checks: integration (kind restore smoke): pass (rerun once due to transient timeout)
source.review_conversation: Ethan Park: The ordering looks good, but we need a hard stop if CRDs aren’t present. We cannot attempt to apply the control-plane chart before CRDs exist (this is what bit us on-prem). Also please ensure we fail fast with an actionable message (which CRD group/version is missing). Benji Okafor: Agreed—added a CRD preflight step that checks the required GVKs and prints the exact missing resources. Also added `--skip-crd-check` (hidden) for internal dev only; default is strict. Sean Gallagher: Can we make sure the report is something Support can ask customers to upload without leaking secrets? Also: step timings and error categories are key. Benji Okafor: Implemented redaction for env vars/flags that may contain tokens; report includes timings per step and `error_category` (preflight | artifact | restore | validation). Added `--report-redact-level=strict` defaulting to strict. Hanae Suzuki: Please confirm we never write decrypted artifact contents to disk in a temp dir by default. On air-gapped/offline this becomes a footgun. Also how do we handle KMS unavailability during restore? Benji Okafor: Updated decrypt path to stream to the restore consumer; temp files are only used if `--workdir` is set explicitly. For KMS: preflight checks reachability and key access; if KMS is down we fail before touching cluster state. Connor O'Brien: This should produce a stable artifact ID and include installer version/backup format version in the report. Also ensure exit codes are consistent for automation. Benji Okafor: Added `artifact_id`, `backup_format_version`, `installer_version`, and standardized exit codes (2=preflight, 3=artifact, 4=restore, 5=validation). Fatima Noor: On-prem edge case: missing CRDs after a cluster rebuild is common; the runbook expects the installer to provide a clear remediation step. Benji Okafor: Added remediation hints to the CRD check output and linked to the internal runbook section in the CLI message. Final review: Ethan approved after CRD check and exit code cleanup; Sean approved after report redaction review; Hanae approved after streaming decrypt change.

source.title: Support air-gapped backup artifact import/export (offline mode + local checksum verification)
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.release_notes: Private installer: add offline import/export helpers for backup artifacts (air-gapped). Includes local SHA256 verification, path safety checks, and docs for deterministic bundle layout.
source.body: ## Summary This PR adds first-class support for moving backup artifacts into/out of air-gapped environments with deterministic, locally verifiable integrity checks. It introduces: (1) `backup export` to package a previously created backup artifact + manifest into a portable bundle directory (or tar), (2) `backup import` to ingest a bundle into an air-gapped target cluster/operator workstation without any outbound calls, and (3) an explicit `--offline` mode that hard-fails if code paths attempt network access (including accidental cloud metadata calls). The goal is to enable on-prem/air-gapped customers to follow a repeatable workflow: create backup -> export to removable media -> import on restore site -> restore apply. Related: ENG-9829 (air-gapped bundle signing/verification follow-ups), ENG-9821 (`backup create`), customer issue context in SUP-18477 (restore failures due to missing/incorrectly staged artifacts). ## Motivation Air-gapped environments frequently have: no DNS resolution, blocked outbound egress, restricted IAM/no cloud credentials, and strict requirements for offline verification (checksums/signatures) prior to use. Prior `backup create` output was usable, but the workflow required manual copying and ad-hoc verification, and parts of the installer could still attempt network access depending on storage backend configuration. ## Design / Behavior - Bundle format: a directory containing `manifest.json` plus the encrypted payload(s) referenced by the manifest. The manifest includes: toolkit version, backup format version, created timestamp, file list, and SHA256 checksums per file. - `backup export --backup-id <id> --out <dir|tar>`: copies artifacts from the local cache/artifact store into the output bundle, writes/updates manifest checksums, and prints a copy-pasteable verification command. - `backup import --from <dir|tar> --into <cache>`: validates manifest schema, verifies checksums, enforces path traversal guards (no `..`, no absolute paths), and then stages files into the installer cache for subsequent `restore apply`. - `--offline` mode: disables any remote storage resolvers and fails fast if a backend requiring network is configured. Also disables AWS IMDS probing and any default credential chain that triggers metadata calls. - Local verification: `backup verify --bundle <dir>` (new subcommand) verifies checksums and prints a summary suitable for attaching to a ticket/escalation. ## Operator workflow example 1) On source site: `redwood-private-installer backup create --output s3://...` (or local) 2) Export: `redwood-private-installer backup export --backup-id 2025-02-06T021533Z --out ./redwood-backup-2025-02-06/` 3) Transfer directory via approved offline mechanism 4) On restore site (air-gapped): `redwood-private-installer backup import --from ./redwood-backup-2025-02-06/ --offline` 5) Restore: `redwood-private-installer restore apply --backup-id 2025-02-06T021533Z --offline` ## Implementation notes - Introduced `pkg/offline/bundle` helpers: manifest read/write, sha256 streaming, copy with fsync, atomic temp file writes. - Added `--offline` flag plumbing into the storage resolver layer; in offline mode we only permit `file://` URIs and local bundle sources. - Added explicit guardrails to prevent accidentally reading from `s3://` or `gs://` during import/verify when `--offline` is set. - Added unit tests for checksum verification, manifest schema validation, and path traversal rejection. - Added lightweight docs under `docs/airgapped-backup-restore.md` describing bundle layout, verification, and troubleshooting. ## Files / areas changed - `cmd/backup_export.go` (new) - `cmd/backup_import.go` (new) - `cmd/backup_verify.go` (new) - `pkg/offline/bundle/*` (new) - `pkg/storage/resolver.go` (offline gating) - `pkg/security/checksums.go` (sha256 helpers) - `docs/airgapped-backup-restore.md` (guidance + FAQ) - `docs/cli.md` (command reference updates) ## Testing - Unit: `go test ./...` includes new bundle tests - Manual: exported/imported bundle on a laptop with `REDWOOD_INSTALLER_OFFLINE=1` and verified no outbound calls (validated with `tcpdump` showing no SYNs, and by forcing DNS failure). - Negative tests: corrupted payload -> checksum mismatch; manifest missing file -> fails; malicious path `../../etc/passwd` -> rejected. ## Follow-ups (not included) - Bundle signing + signature verification (GPG/cosign) will be done in ENG-9829; this PR only covers checksums to close the immediate workflow gap. - Integrate `backup import` into the on-prem offline installer bundle (Release Engineering). ## Checklist - [x] No external calls in offline flow - [x] Checksum verification + clear operator output - [x] Docs for air-gapped workflow - [x] Tests for traversal + corruption cases
commits: cmd: add backup export command and bundle writer
cmd: add backup import + staging into local cache
cmd: add backup verify for offline integrity checks
storage: enforce offline mode restrictions and disable metadata probing
docs: air-gapped backup/import workflow and troubleshooting
tests: manifest validation, sha256 verification, path traversal guards
review_conversation: Connor O'Brien: Can we guarantee `--offline` doesn’t accidentally hit AWS IMDS via the default credential chain? We’ve been burned by this in CI and in locked-down customer networks (hangs ~2s per attempt).

Benji Okafor (author): Good call. Added explicit `DisableIMDS` toggle in the resolver when offline and a fast-fail if any non-file backend is configured. Also added a unit test that asserts offline mode rejects `s3://` even when creds exist.

Hanae Suzuki: Checksum verification is necessary but not sufficient for tamper resistance. Please make sure docs are clear that checksums only detect corruption unless the manifest is signed. Also: import must guard against path traversal / absolute paths.

Benji Okafor (author): Updated docs with a dedicated note: checksums detect corruption; signing is follow-up ENG-9829. Added path sanitation: reject absolute paths, `..` segments, and enforce extraction into a temp dir with atomic moves.

Fatima Noor: On-prem operators need a very explicit copy/paste verification command and a deterministic bundle layout. Also, can we support both directory and tarball? Some customers only allow moving a single file.

Benji Okafor (author): Added `--out <dir>` and `--out <file>.tar` support; same for `--from`. The CLI prints a suggested `backup verify --bundle ...` command and shows a short summary (bytes, file count, sha256 status).
Sean Gallagher: For SRE smoke tests, we’ll want a machine-readable verification report (json) so we can attach it to staging restore drill outputs. Can we add `--format json`?
Benji Okafor (author): Added `backup verify --format text|json` (default text). JSON output is a flat structure with per-file results; also includes `tool_version` and `backup_format_version`.
Connor O'Brien: CI failed on first run due to lint and an unused import; please fix and rerun. Also update `docs/cli.md` to include new subcommands.
Benji Okafor (author): Fixed lint, removed unused import, updated CLI docs. Re-ran CI (all green).
Hanae Suzuki: Security LGTM with the docs caveat + traversal guard.
Fatima Noor: LGTM; this unblocks the MedData-style air-gap flow. Please coordinate with Release Eng for the offline bundle packaging once ENG-9829 lands.
Connor O'Brien: Approved. Let’s make sure release notes call out the new commands and offline behavior.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main by Connor O'Brien. Squash commit message: "Support air-gapped backup artifact import/export (offline mode + local checksum verification)"
