Prior `backup create` output was usable, but the workflow required manual copying and ad-hoc verification, and parts of the installer could still attempt network access depending on storage backend configuration. ## Design / Behavior - Bundle format: a directory containing `manifest.json` plus the encrypted payload(s) referenced by the manifest. The manifest includes: toolkit version, backup format version, created timestamp, file list, and SHA256 checksums per file. - `backup export --backup-id <id> --out <dir|tar>`: copies artifacts from the local cache/artifact store into the output bundle, writes/updates manifest checksums, and prints a copy-pasteable verification command. - `backup import --from <dir|tar> --into <cache>`: validates manifest schema, verifies checksums, enforces path traversal guards (no `..`, no absolute paths), and then stages files into the installer cache for subsequent `restore apply`. - `--offline` mode: disables any remote storage resolvers and fails fast if a backend requiring network is configured. Also disables AWS IMDS probing and any default credential chain that triggers metadata calls. - Local verification: `backup verify --bundle <dir>` (new subcommand) verifies checksums and prints a summary suitable for attaching to a ticket/escalation. ## Operator workflow example 1) On source site: `redwood-private-installer backup create --output s3://...` (or local) 2) Export: `redwood-private-installer backup export --backup-id 2025-02-06T021533Z --out ./redwood-backup-2025-02-06/` 3) Transfer directory via approved offline mechanism 4) On restore site (air-gapped): `redwood-private-installer backup import --from ./redwood-backup-2025-02-06/ --offline` 5) Restore: `redwood-private-installer restore apply --backup-id 2025-02-06T021533Z --offline` ## Implementation notes - Introduced `pkg/offline/bundle` helpers: manifest read/write, sha256 streaming, copy with fsync, atomic temp file writes. - Added `--offline` flag plumbing into the storage resolver layer; in offline mode we only permit `file://` URIs and local bundle sources. - Added explicit guardrails to prevent accidentally reading from `s3://` or `gs://` during import/verify when `--offline` is set. - Added unit tests for checksum verification, manifest schema validation, and path traversal rejection. - Added lightweight docs under `docs/airgapped-backup-restore.md` describing bundle layout, verification, and troubleshooting. ## Files / areas changed - `cmd/backup_export.go` (new) - `cmd/backup_import.go` (new) - `cmd/backup_verify.go` (new) - `pkg/offline/bundle/*` (new) - `pkg/storage/resolver.go` (offline gating) - `pkg/security/checksums.go` (sha256 helpers) - `docs/airgapped-backup-restore.md` (guidance + FAQ) - `docs/cli.md` (command reference updates) ## Testing - Unit: `go test ./...` includes new bundle tests - Manual: exported/imported bundle on a laptop with `REDWOOD_INSTALLER_OFFLINE=1` and verified no outbound calls (validated with `tcpdump` showing no SYNs, and by forcing DNS failure). - Negative tests: corrupted payload -> checksum mismatch; manifest missing file -> fails; malicious path `../../etc/passwd` -> rejected. ## Follow-ups (not included) - Bundle signing + signature verification (GPG/cosign) will be done in ENG-9829; this PR only covers checksums to close the immediate workflow gap. - Integrate `backup import` into the on-prem offline installer bundle (Release Engineering). ## Checklist - [x] No external calls in offline flow - [x] Checksum verification + clear operator output - [x] Docs for air-gapped workflow - [x] Tests for traversal + corruption cases

source.title: Add envelope encryption KMS provider interface (AWS reference impl + secure defaults)
source.author: Hanae Suzuki
source.description:
Context: Private Day-2 backup/restore requires encryption at rest with customer-managed keys where applicable. The installer currently produces backup artifacts (tarball + manifest), but encryption is not pluggable and we can’t support customer KMS/HSM constraints cleanly across VPC + on-prem. This PR introduces a small, testable abstraction for KMS-based envelope encryption and ships a single reference provider implementation for AWS KMS, with secure defaults and explicit failure modes.

What’s in this PR: (1) New `kms.Provider` interface (GenerateDataKey/DecryptDataKey) that supports KMS-backed wrapping/unwrapping of a per-backup DEK (data encryption key). (2) New `crypto/envelope` package that performs streaming AES-256-GCM encryption of backup payloads using a DEK, storing only the wrapped key + nonce + algorithm metadata in the backup manifest. (3) AWS reference provider using `kms.GenerateDataKey`/`kms.Decrypt` with enforced `EncryptionContext` (backup_id, cluster_id, artifact_kind, format_version). (4) Secure defaults: AES-256-GCM, random 96-bit nonce, fail-closed if KMS is configured but unavailable, and redaction of any key material in logs/errors. (5) Tests: deterministic fake provider, manifest round-trip tests, and a “wrong context” decrypt failure test to prevent accidental weakening.

Non-goals (explicit): This PR does not wire envelope encryption into `backup create` yet (that’s handled in follow-up in ENG-9821/ENG-9823 integration work). This also does not attempt to support every KMS flavor immediately; the interface is designed so we can add GCP KMS / Azure Key Vault / on-prem HSM providers incrementally without touching encryption logic.

Operator-facing behavior: When enabled, backup artifacts include `encryption` metadata in `manifest.json` and the payload is emitted as `payload.enc` instead of `payload.tar.gz`. Restore requires access to the same KMS key (or a key that can decrypt the wrapped DEK) and the same encryption context fields. AWS provider configuration is via flags/env: `--kms-provider=aws`, `--kms-key-arn=...`, optional `--kms-region=...`. If `--kms-provider` is set, the tool will refuse to proceed without a key ARN.

Security notes: We do not store plaintext DEKs anywhere on disk; DEKs live in-memory for the encryption session. Wrapped keys are stored in the manifest (base64). We rely on KMS for KEK lifecycle/rotation. We intentionally treat KMS failures as hard failures (no silent downgrade) to avoid producing unencrypted backups unexpectedly.

Follow-ups: (a) integrate into backup/restore commands + Helm CronJob values (ENG-9823/ENG-9824), (b) document IAM least-privilege policy for AWS KMS usage (ENG-9825), (c) add “provider health” preflight for scheduled backups so we fail early with actionable errors.
commits: security/kms: add Provider interface and common errors
crypto/envelope: implement AES-256-GCM envelope encryption + manifest metadata
kms/aws: add AWS KMS provider (GenerateDataKey/Decrypt) w/ encryption context
tests: add fake provider + manifest round-trip tests
docs: add configuration notes and troubleshooting section
changed_areas: cmd/private-installer (shared flags wiring for kms config; no behavior change unless enabled)
internal/kms (provider interface, aws provider, fake provider for tests)
docs/backup-restore (initial operator notes + AWS permissions callouts)
notable_snippets: Go interface introduced: `type Provider interface { GenerateDataKey(ctx context.Context, keyID string, encContext map[string]string) (plaintextDEK []byte, wrappedDEK []byte, err error); DecryptDataKey(ctx context.Context, keyID string, wrappedDEK []byte, encContext map[string]string) (plaintextDEK []byte, err error) }`

Manifest fields (new): `encryption.algorithm=aes-256-gcm`, `encryption.kms_provider=aws`, `encryption.kms_key_id=<arn>`, `encryption.wrapped_dek_b64=...`, `encryption.nonce_b64=...`, `encryption.context={backup_id,cluster_id,artifact_kind,format_version}` (context is validated but never treated as secret).
review_conversation: Marcus Lin: Can we confirm we never log the wrapped key / plaintext key bytes? Also, what’s our stance on KMS outage (fail open vs fail closed)?

Hanae Suzuki (author): Confirmed: we only log provider type + key ARN suffix (last 6) and backup_id; wrapped/plaintext key material is redacted. This PR fails closed if `--kms-provider` is set. I added a test that asserts error strings don’t include base64 blobs and a guard that prevents “plaintext fallback”.
Ethan Park: Please ensure encryption context is consistent and versioned; we’ll need it stable for restore. Also add a check that context mismatch errors are actionable.

Hanae Suzuki (author): Added `format_version` into context and a typed `ErrDecryptDenied` with a hint to verify key + context fields. The context keys are constants in `internal/kms/context.go` to avoid drift.
Connor O'Brien: For air-gapped bundles, AWS provider will not be usable; does the interface force AWS SDK dependencies into all builds?

Hanae Suzuki (author): AWS provider is in `internal/kms/aws` and only linked when selected; base binary still includes the code but configuration defaults to `none`. No network calls unless provider is enabled. If we need to exclude AWS SDK for certain builds later, we can split with build tags (not in this PR).
Sean Gallagher: We should have a fast-fail preflight for scheduled backups so CronJobs don’t run for 20 minutes then error. Not blocking, but please track.
Hanae Suzuki (author): Agreed; called out as follow-up. This PR keeps scope to interface + crypto primitives.
Sahana Iyer: CI green. Please add a brief note to the changelog for Private installer security features.
Hanae Suzuki (author): Added a short release note under “Backup/Restore”.
Marcus Lin: Approved with the fail-closed behavior and context validation.
Ethan Park: Approved.
Connor O'Brien: Approved.
release_notes: Private installer: added KMS envelope encryption primitives and AWS KMS provider interface support (not enabled by default).
Backup artifacts can now carry encryption metadata for future backup/restore integration.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main; commit message: "Add envelope encryption KMS provider interface (AWS reference impl + secure defaults)"

source.title: Add upgrade preflight gate + rollback hints for enterprise private upgrades
source.author: Elliot Price
source.description:
Context: Private upgrades were relying on tribal knowledge for preflight checks and rollback readiness. This PR adds a first-class `upgrade preflight` command and standardizes the minimum gates we expect before an enterprise window starts. The intent is to align the tool output with the Confluence go/no-go checklist and the Change Management policy.

What changed: (1) New command `rpi upgrade preflight` that runs cluster readiness, API deprecation checks, cert expiry checks, and audit-log sink reachability checks. (2) Adds `--evidence-file` flag to dump a text report for attachment to a Jira change record. (3) Adds inline rollback hints based on what was changed (control plane vs data plane).

Operator-facing behavior: The preflight exits non-zero on hard blockers. Warnings are printed for non-blocking findings and are intended to be explicitly acknowledged by the change owner.

Checklist: [x] Docs updated (README) [x] Unit tests [x] Manual test on staging EKS cluster [x] Security review requested

Links: Confluence: Private Deployments: Upgrade Go/No-Go Checklist (Enterprise); Confluence: Production Change Management Policy; Confluence: Secure SDLC: Security Sign-off for Customer Deployments.
source.conversation:
Grace O'Connor: Can we ensure the preflight includes an error-budget note? Even if Private isn't on our public SLOs, we should link to the planned-change review.
Elliot Price: Added a section that prints: "Confirm SLO/error budget review completed per policy" and links to the doc in the help text.
Naomi Feldman: For the audit sink reachability test, do we risk exposing customer endpoints in logs?
Elliot Price: The evidence file redacts hostnames by default, prints only "reachable/unreachable" and a correlation id.
Ethan Park: Please add a deterministic rollback hint for the operator/controller chart.
Elliot Price: Added: "Rollback: rerun installer with --version <previous> and --restore-values <path>" plus a note about validating reconciliation health.
commits_summary: checks: cert expiry + deprecated API detector
docs: update README with enterprise go/no-go mapping

source.title: private-installer: add `backup create` command (manifest + encrypted artifact + verification output)
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.description: This PR introduces an initial `backup create` workflow in the Redwood Private installer CLI to support Day-2 operations and DR readiness. The command produces (1) a human-readable manifest (JSON) describing what was captured and how it was encrypted, and (2) an encrypted artifact (tarball) containing the control-plane state snapshot files and selected deterministic configuration inputs. The goal of this PR is to ship the core mechanics (consistent snapshot + packaging + encryption + basic verification/logging) so Solutions/SRE teams can begin rehearsing restores in staging while we iterate on storage backends, scheduled jobs, and restore automation. Scope: control-plane backup only (no data-plane model cache / no customer prompt data). Secrets are not exported as raw secret material; we only record references (e.g., external KMS key ARNs, secret names) and require the target cluster to have access to the same secret sources at restore time. The artifact format is intentionally versioned (`format_version: 1`) and includes integrity checks (SHA256) for each included file and the encrypted blob. Encryption is implemented as AES-256-GCM with a randomly generated data key; for this PR, the data key is derived from a user-provided passphrase via scrypt and stored only in-memory during packaging (no plaintext key material written to disk). A follow-up will add a KMS provider interface for envelope encryption (see ENG-9823) and backup storage backends (S3/NFS) integration. Operator UX: `redwood-private backup create --output ./backups --cluster-context <ctx> [--include-etcd-snapshot=auto] [--quiesce-timeout=60s]` prints a summary, writes `manifest.json` and `backup-<cluster>-<ts>.tar.enc`, and optionally runs a post-backup verification step that re-reads the artifact header and validates checksums. Logging: adds structured log lines with `backup_id`, `duration_ms`, `bytes_written`, and `error_category` for future metrics wiring. Documentation: adds CLI help text and a short internal README section describing the artifact layout and the restore prerequisites. Known limitations (explicitly documented in help output): (a) does not back up Kubernetes resources/CRDs (Velero integration is tracked separately), (b) etcd snapshot capture is best-effort and gated behind `--include-etcd-snapshot` because some Private installs do not grant direct etcd access, (c) no remote upload; output is a local directory only, (d) no key rotation / KMS-based sealing yet. This aligns with ADR-027 direction (manifest + encrypted bundle + deterministic layout), but the key management details are expected to change once KMS provider lands; the format_version is reserved for that evolution.
commits: cli: add `backup` command group + `backup create` subcommand
backup: implement snapshot planner for control-plane components
backup: add artifact writer (tar + manifest + checksums) and encryptor
logging: standardize backup/restore log fields + error categories
docs: add installer README section for backup artifact layout
tests: add unit tests for manifest schema + checksum verification
source.testing: Unit tests: `go test ./...` (added focused tests for manifest serialization, checksum generation, and decrypt+verify path using a fixed nonce in test-only helper). Manual: validated on staging cluster `stg-private-3` by running `redwood-private backup create --output /tmp/rw-backups --include-etcd-snapshot=off` and confirming (1) artifact + manifest written, (2) summary printed, (3) `--verify` reports PASS, (4) failure modes produce actionable error_category (permissions, missing kubecontext, insufficient disk).
review_conversation: Ethan Park: The include list mentions `routing-policies`. Where are those sourced from? We should be explicit that we’re backing up control-plane DB state + installer-known config, not live K8s objects. Also please add a format_version to the manifest so we can evolve encryption without breaking restores.

Benji Okafor (author): Routing policies are persisted in the config DB and surfaced via the control-plane API; this implementation snapshots the DB, and the manifest component list is descriptive (not a source-of-truth inventory). I added `format_version: 1` and clarified in help text that K8s resources are out of scope for this command.

Sean Gallagher: For on-call usability, we need deterministic filenames and a stable location for `latest`. Also, log output should include duration and bytes; future dashboards will rely on it. Can we also avoid printing anything that looks like key material?

Benji Okafor (author): Added deterministic filename template `backup-<cluster>-<timestamp>.tar.enc` and `manifest-<backup_id>.json`. I held off on a `latest` symlink because Windows/on-prem customers may not have it; instead we print the output paths and backup_id. Logging now includes `duration_ms`/`bytes_written` and we never log salt/nonce or passphrase-derived data.

Hanae Suzuki: Please double-check the crypto: AES-GCM nonce reuse would be catastrophic. Make sure nonce is random per artifact and not derived from timestamp. Also, can we add guardrails so the artifact never includes raw Kubernetes Secret values?

Benji Okafor (author): Nonce is generated from `crypto/rand` per artifact; tests use fixed nonce in a test-only helper. Added an explicit denylist in the planner: we will not read `Secret` data from the cluster; we only snapshot application datastores + config files and record secret *references* in the manifest when applicable.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging question: we’ll need this to work in air-gapped flows later. Please keep the output fully self-contained, and don’t add implicit network calls. Also, ensure we can verify integrity offline.

Benji Okafor (author): No network calls in this PR. Verification is offline: we hash files pre-encryption, hash the encrypted blob, and emit those in the manifest. `--verify` reads the artifact header and validates checksums without external dependencies.

Kira Thompson: The CLI help reads like a runbook; keep it concise but include prerequisites and a link to the Confluence runbook once it exists. Also, add an example command with `--quiesce-timeout` since that’s a common customer ask.
Benji Okafor (author): Updated help text to a concise prerequisites list + example commands, and added TODO link placeholder for Confluence page (will be replaced when docs land).
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into `main` by Connor O'Brien after approvals from Ethan Park and Sean Gallagher. Squash commit message: "Add backup create command to private installer (manifest + encrypted artifact)".
source.release_notes: Private installer now supports `redwood-private backup create` to generate a versioned manifest and encrypted backup artifact suitable for staging DR rehearsals. No external storage backends or KMS envelope encryption in this release.

This aligns with ADR-027 direction (manifest + encrypted bundle + deterministic layout), but the key management details are expected to change once KMS provider lands; the format_version is reserved for that evolution.

source.title: add-oci-prefetcher-and-kubelet-ephemeral-overlay-for-airgapped-bootstrap
source.author: Diego Morales
source.release_notes: Adds an OCI prefetcher service and a minimal ephemeral kubelet overlay for faster air-gapped bootstrap. Backward compatible; no action required for existing installs.
source.description:
Problem: air-gapped installs often spend excessive time pulling large runtime images and kernel modules during initial bootstrap. Existing delta snapshots help, but cluster bring-up still faces long cold starts and fragile manual registry seeding.

Solution: introduce an OCI prefetcher + index format plus a thin ephemeral kubelet overlay that allows the installer to stream and stage only required image layers and kubelet artifacts into a temporary local store before handing control to the normal runtime. Key components in this PR: (1) installer/terraform module to create a prefetcher DaemonSet and registry proxy for sealed bundles; (2) a compact OCI index format and tooling (installer/tools/oci-indexer) that produces layered prefetch manifests optimized for minimal disk churn; (3) helm chart additions that render a kubelet-ephemeral overlay (charts/redwood-private/templates/kubelet-ephemeral.yaml) and init hooks to populate an overlayfs during airgap bootstrap; (4) preflight + smoke tests and docs for operators.

What changed (high level):
- Add installer/tools/oci-indexer and a small Go binary that walks OCI archives and emits prefetch manifests containing ordered layer digests and platform hints.
- Add installer/scripts/prefetch-service.sh and a lightweight systemd unit template used by the bootstrapper to start an ephemeral prefetcher when offline caches are empty.
- Terraform: installer/terraform/modules/prefetcher - module to deploy the prefetcher, registry proxy, and RBAC artifacts for private installs.
- Helm: charts/redwood-private chart + values to opt into ephemeral kubelet overlay and prefetcher sidecar (default OFF).
- Installer: hook to consume index manifests and drive parallel layer download with an adaptive concurrency limiter to avoid OOM on small nodes.
- Docs: docs/airgap/prefetcher.md with operator instructions and upgrade notes.

Checklist: 
- [x] Add prefetcher binary + tests
- [x] Terraform module with example usage
- [x] Helm templates and values gating
- [x] Integration smoke test for airgap bootstrap
- [x] Docs and upgrade notes

Compatibility and rollout:
- Feature gated (values.prefetcher.enabled=false by default) so existing clusters are unaffected.
- Installer will use the prefetcher automatically when values.prefetcher.enabled=true and a sealed-bundle index is provided.

Notes on safety: prefetcher runs as non-privileged user with limited caps; the ephemeral kubelet overlay uses overlayfs mounted to a temporary path and automatically unmounted after promotion. We included an explicit rollback hook that removes staged layers if the bootstrap fails, avoiding disk pollution.

Merge: Squash-merged to main after two approvals and green CI.

Related: Linear ENG-8844 tracks follow-ups for multi-arch prefetch manifests and stronger integrity checking. SUP-2201 is the support ticket that motivated the work (customer reported 2+ hour cold bootstrap on baremetal with strict egress).
commits: 7a4b2d1: add oci-indexer tool and unit tests
b9dc88f: terraform module for prefetcher + registry-proxy
e3f02a5: helm chart: kubelet-ephemeral overlay and prefetcher sidecar templates
c1f79b0: installer: hook to apply prefetch manifest and stage layers
d4a7e11: scripts: prefetch service unit and bootstrap integration
f29b3c7: docs: airgap prefetcher operator guide and upgrade notes
9c8b4d2: tests: integration smoke test for airgap prefetch flow
review_comments: Ava Chen: Nice start—can you add an opt-out env var for constrained nodes? (requested change)
Diego Morales: Added PREFETCH_MAX_CONCURRENCY with a sane default and made it configurable via values.yaml (reply)
Priya Nair: Please add a unit test to cover indexer ordering for multi-platform manifests. There was a flake in CI. (requested change)
Diego Morales: Added unit to confirm ordering and pinned a flaky dependency; reran CI. (reply)
Liam O'Connor: Approving — verified helm templates render in local k3s smoke cluster and rollback hook cleans up layers. (approved)

source.title: manifest rollup + attestation gating: canonical index and CI enforcement
source.author: Maya Patel
source.description:
Motivation: Private-installer releases need a single canonical manifest index that the CI can attest and gate on before publishing to customers. This PR implements a manifest rollup transformer that ingests per-arch/per-release artifact metadata, computes a deterministic canonical index (content-addressed ordering + delta graph), emits an attestable bundle, and wires an optional CI gating check that rejects a release if attestations are missing or provenance fails verification.

This is intended to reduce the number of manual steps in private-installer release runs, make rollbacks deterministic (by publishing a signed index snapshot), and give SREs/Customers cryptographic proof of the exact artifacts applied by an upgrade. Related Linear: ENG-4379 (manifest rollup) and ENG-4391 (attestation policy).

What this PR does (high level):
- Adds manifest-rollup binary and library used in the release pipeline to compose a single canonical manifest index from per-artifact metadata.
- Produces a deterministic index file (index.json.sha256) plus an attestable bundle (index.tar.gz.sig + provenance.json) suitable for cosign/canonical verification.
- Integrates a new CI gating job (release-index-verify) in .github/workflows/release.yml that verifies signatures, provenance chains, and KMS references before allowing the release job to proceed.
- Adds a backfill migration script to regenerate canonical indexes for a small subset of historical releases (used in the migration smoke tests).
- Documents the attestation policy and failure modes in docs/installer/release-attest.md.

Checklist: [x] unit + integration tests for rollup logic, [x] end-to-end CI check wired, [x] docs updated, [x] security review acknowledged.

Notes: The design favors deterministic ordering over human-readable diffs (index is content-addressed). The attestation policy is configurable per-env (see installer/release-attest.md). If the KMS path is unavailable the gate fails closed; we ship a temporary bypass keyed to a Linear ticket for emergency rollouts but it is disabled by default.
commits: a1f9c3b: feat(manifest-rollup): new rollup transformer and deterministic index writer
b4e2d7a: test(manifest-rollup): unit tests for ordering and delta graph generation
c2d8f11: ci(release): add release-index-verify job to workflow and secrets usage
d9b7e5f: docs(installer): add release-attest.md and operational notes
e6a3c2b: tools: backfill script for regenerating historic canonical indexes
review_comments: Ethan Park: Can we please avoid depending on a single KMS path? Suggested adding a fallback verify-only mode — responded that verify-only exists but the gate fails closed by default.
Sofia Martinez: Tests look good; request small refactor to separate signing from upload side-effects — author split attest.go into attest_sign.go and attest_upload.go in a follow-up commit.
Daniel Kim: Question about index size and memory use for very large releases; added streaming writer + chunked processing to indexer and added integration test that simulates 10k artifact entries.
ci_checks: release/index_verify: pass (verified cosign signatures + provenance chain)
source.merge_details: Merged by squash on 2025-02-02 after CI green and two approvals. Merge commit message lists ENG-4379 and SUP-1284 and includes a short migration plan. Post-merge: CI kickstarted a backfill job for 5 historical release candidates against a staging bucket; no regressions observed. Documented emergency bypass procedure in ATTENTION.md.
release_notes: Private-installer: canonical release index and attestation gating added; release CI now rejects un-attested indexes by default.
Operators: new migration script to backfill canonical indexes for selected historic releases.
Security: index bundles are signed (cosign) and provenance.json is published alongside the index for downstream verification.

source.title: transactional-release-apply: add TUF-backed transactional locks and manifest staging
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description:
Problem: release application for private-installer can partially apply manifests when concurrent pipelines overlap (e.g. two builds push near-simultaneously). This has caused transient mismatches between the published release manifest and the in-cluster helm state, forcing manual rollbacks. Goal: make release manifest publishing and cluster apply transactional and idempotent so that either the entire manifest set is applied (and published) or nothing changes, with a short, observable staging window and an automatic rollback marker if something fails.

What this PR does (single string): Adds a TUF-backed transactional lock and a manifest staging area to the private-installer release pipeline. The new flow stages a release bundle under a provisional manifest name (manifest:vN-staging), obtains a transactional advisory lock via the TUF metadata store (using conditional metadata updates and an ephemeral lock role), performs preflight validation (diff checks, schema/helm lint, and cluster dry-run against the target kubeconfig), attempts a staged helm apply using --wait and a short timeout, and upon success atomically promotes the staged manifest to the canonical manifest name (manifest:vN) using a signed metadata rotation step. If promotion fails or preflight fails, we write a rollback marker file (manifest:vN-rollback) and garbage collect the staged artifacts after a TTL. The implementation is intentionally conservative: timeouts and retries are tunable via pipeline variables and default to conservative values to reduce false positives.

Changes included: 1) CI pipeline: new release-transactional job (gitlab/worker) that stages artifacts and orchestrates TUF metadata updates; 2) TUF tooling: add tuf-lock CLI and small library helpers for conditional role updates and ephemeral lock roles; 3) Installer: manifest staging path support and rollback marker handling in the private-installer repo; 4) Helm: chart hooks updated to treat staged annotations specially and to emit structured apply result records; 5) Terraform: adds a data source that can query the canonical manifest state for automation hooks; 6) Tests: end-to-end CI integration test simulating concurrent publishes, and unit tests around lock edge-cases.

Why this approach: We considered a pure datastore advisory lock but chose to build on top of our existing TUF metadata store to ensure provenance and signed atomicity of promotion steps. This gives auditability (signed metadata changes) and prevents race conditions across regions that rely on the same artifact index. The staged manifest approach also preserves a traceable artifact lineage for rollbacks and postmortem.

Checklist: - [x] Add staged manifest path and promotion API - [x] Implement tuf-lock CLI + library - [x] Hook promotion to signed metadata rotation - [x] Add rollback marker and GC policy - [x] Integration tests for concurrent publish - [x] Update release docs and runbook snippets - [x] CI job and RBAC for pipeline to sign/push metadata

Notes and migration: Existing release consumers that read manifest:vN remain unchanged. New consumers can query manifest:vN-staging when they want to validate in a staging environment. The pipeline introduces two new environment variables (RELEASE_LOCK_TTL and STAGING_TTL) with reasonable defaults. We also rotate one ephemeral TUF key used for lock leases; the rotation step is included and gated behind a feature flag for the first rollout.
commits: a1e7b9c: add tuf-lock library and CLI
b2d3c4f: pipeline job for transactional release apply
c3f4e5a: integrate staged manifest promotion into installer apply flow
d4e5f6b: helm chart: annotate staged resources and emit apply records
e5f6a7c: terraform: add data source to read manifest state
f6a7b8d: tests: concurrent publish integration test harness
review_comments: Diego Morales: We should be careful about TUF metadata size growth with staging metadata — can we compact signed history after promotion? (answered: we add a compaction GC that keeps N historical signed promotions and tombstones older ones)
Anya Patel: The helm --wait timeout default seems long for CI. Suggest adding a short default and exposing config. (fixed: exposed STAGING_TIMEOUT and reduced default to 3m)

Owen Park: Race scenario where promotion completes but a downstream CDN hasn't pulled new blob; how do consumers handle eventual consistency? (reply: producers must write promotion + published index; consumers can use a small backoff or detect 404s and retry; added doc section)
Security review (Emily Zhao): Ensure pipeline signing keys are in KMS and not present in CI image; verify — switched to KMS sign flow and added an allowlist for signing service accounts
ci_checks: integration: fail (first run - discovered race in promotions)
source.release_notes: Adds transactional release apply for private-installer: staged manifests, TUF-backed advisory locks, promotion and rollback markers, and CI job to orchestrate atomic publishes. Improves reliability for concurrent releases and provides stronger provenance for promotion steps.

source.title: implement-tf-module-gpu-nodepool-lease-index-and-iam-role-template-rotation
source.author: Leah Morales
source.release_notes: Add a lease-indexed GPU nodepool TF module and introduce immutable IAM role templating with rotation hooks; includes migration tool and rollout docs.
source.description:
Context and motivation: Private deployments need consistent, deterministic allocation of GPU nodepools across tenants while preserving immutability in IAM role configuration for auditability. Current tenant lease allocation can drift when nodes are recreated or TF state is sharded, causing tenant-to-node affinity regressions and noisy autoscaler behavior. This PR introduces a new Terraform module that provides a deterministic `lease_index` allocator for GPU nodepools and an IAM role templating system that creates immutable role versions with automated rotation hooks.

What this PR does: 1) Adds module `tfmodules/gpu_lease_index` implementing a lease-index allocation strategy that: computes a stable index for tenant leases based on tenant-id hashing + ordered subnet slices; emits outputs consumed by the nodepool provisioning module to ensure stable assignment even during partial applies and state sharding. 2) Adds module `tfmodules/iam_role_template` which renders IAM role artifacts as immutable-versioned resources (role_v1, role_v2) and wires a controlled rotation mechanism via a small controller job (K8s Job manifest + preflight plan) that can be driven by `private-installer upgrade`. 3) Provides a migration tool `scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate` (dry-run + apply modes) to compute and persist lease_index mappings into a new state namespace, with safety checks and a --rollback snapshot option. 4) Adds tagging for GPU nodepool instances to surface lease_index and role_version for drift detection and reconciler diagnostics. 5) Updates Helm hooks in `charts/private-installer` to add post-upgrade validation for IAM rotations and nodepool tag presence.

Design notes: - Lease index derivation uses a stable HMAC(nonce=deployment-id, key=infra-hash) truncated to the pool size to ensure stable ordering across applies. The TF module exposes both `lease_index` and `lease_salt` so downstream modules can detect mismatches and trigger safe rebinds. - IAM role templating intentionally creates new role resources instead of in-place edits; the rotation controller sequences traffic to roles by annotating service accounts, running preflight checks, then promoting a new version, and finally deleting the old role only when safe. - The migration tool does not alter live node labels; it only writes the lease_index mapping into the TF state file and produces a reconciliation plan for the installer to apply.

title: implement-tf-module-gpu-nodepool-lease-index-and-iam-role-template-rotation
preview:
Context and motivation: Private deployments need consistent, deterministic allocation of GPU nodepools across tenants while preserving immutability in IAM role configuration for auditability. Current tenant lease allocation can drift when nodes are recreated or TF state is sharded, causing tenant-to-node affinity regressions and noisy autoscaler behavior. This PR introduces a new Terraform module that provides a deterministic `lease_index` allocator for GPU nodepools and an IAM role templating system that creates immutable role versions with automated rotation hooks.

What this PR does: 1) Adds module `tfmodules/gpu_lease_index` implementing a lease-index allocation strategy that: computes a stable index for tenant leases based on tenant-id hashing + ordered subnet slices; emits outputs consumed by the nodepool provisioning module to ensure stable assignment even during partial applies and state sharding. 2) Adds module `tfmodules/iam_role_template` which renders IAM role artifacts as immutable-versioned resources (role_v1, role_v2) and wires a controlled rotation mechanism via a small controller job (K8s Job manifest + preflight plan) that can be driven by `private-installer upgrade`. 3) Provides a migration tool `scripts/tfstate-lease-index-migrate` (dry-run + apply modes) to compute and persist lease_index mappings into a new state namespace, with safety checks and a --rollback snapshot option. 4) Adds tagging for GPU nodepool instances to surface lease_index and role_version for drift detection and reconciler diagnostics. 5) Updates Helm hooks in `charts/private-installer` to add post-upgrade validation for IAM rotations and nodepool tag presence.

Design notes: - Lease index derivation uses a stable HMAC(nonce=deployment-id, key=infra-hash) truncated to the pool size to ensure stable ordering across applies. The TF module exposes both `lease_index` and `lease_salt` so downstream modules can detect mismatches and trigger safe rebinds. - IAM role templating intentionally creates new role resources instead of in-place edits; the rotation controller sequences traffic to roles by annotating service accounts, running preflight checks, then promoting a new version, and finally deleting the old role only when safe. - The migration tool does not alter live node labels; it only writes the lease_index mapping into the TF state file and produces a reconciliation plan for the installer to apply.

source.title: introduce-interactive-migration-safepoints-and-rollback-fence
source.author: Anjali Kapoor
source.description:
Motivation: Private deployments need a safer, operator-driven way to perform schema migrations and rollbacks across multi-tenant clusters. Previous upgrades occasionally left clusters in a state where a naive downgrade could break tenant DB schemas or leave a partial logical replication setup. This PR introduces (1) an interactive safepoint API exposed by the operator to mark tenant migration safepoints, (2) a rollback-fence that prevents downgrades when incompatible schema delta exists, and (3) a set of Helm and Terraform guard checks to ensure plan/upgrade parity before applying changes.

Summary of changes (single-line): interactive safepoint operator API, rollback-fence enforcement, helm preflight hook to quiesce workloads, timeline-aware postgres snapshotter and restore verifier, terraform state version gate.

Checklist (for merge):
- [x] Operator: add Safepoint CRD + controller to record migration checkpoints and upstream WAL position
- [x] Installer: implement rollback-fence logic that reads last safepoint and schema-compat index
- [x] Helm chart: add pre-upgrade hook that quiesces user-facing processes and snapshots logical replication slots
- [x] Terraform: add plan-time check that validates cluster manifest hash and fails apply if state version mismatches
- [x] E2E: add integration test that performs upgrade -> set safepoint -> simulate downgrade attempt -> assert fence blocks downgrade
- [x] Docs: write runbook for interactive safepoint operations and emergency rollback flow

Detailed description: The operator now exposes a new CRD called RedwoodMigrationSafepoint which operators (or the CLI) can create to mark a tenant-aware migration checkpoint. Each safepoint records a cluster-scoped manifest hash, postgres timeline ID, LSN, and an optional human message. The safepoint is written atomically and also persisted to the installer metadata store. During upgrades, the operator will record an automatic pre-migration safepoint (when enabled) and annotate it into the release metadata. The rollback-fence consults the latest safepoint and a lightweight schema-compatibility index (stored as a manifest label) before permitting any Helm/Terraform-driven downgrade. If the fence detects a schema delta that cannot be auto-downgraded, it rejects the operation with an actionable error and links to the runbook.

Helm changes: added hooks/ pre-upgrade-job.yaml which calls operator apis: operatorctl quiesce --release <release> --timeout 5m and operatorctl snapshot --tenant <tenant> to produce a consistent logical snapshot. Hook is non-blocking by default but can be forced via values: preflight.quiesce=true. Also added post-rollback-validation hook that waits for WAL replay and checksum verification.

Terraform changes: new module variable manifest_lock_hash and pre-apply validation that calls the installer API for expected state. A sentinel file is written to the cluster object store as a short-lived plan-lease; terraform apply will fail if another active plan-lease exists or if the manifest hash differs. This prevents divergent state.apply sequences across CI and manual runs.

Postgres snapshotter & restore: add timeline-aware logical snapshotter which captures timeline ID and LSN alongside snapshot tarball. Restore verifier replays WAL up to the LSN and then runs a fast integrity check (row counts + schema checksums) for configured critical tables. The restore path includes a --dry-verify mode for customers to run a low-risk restore verification before committing.

Runbook & UX: documented operator CLI verbs: safepoint create/list/delete, operatorctl fence-status, operatorctl force-unlock (requires 2 approvals). The runbook includes step-by-step rollback example: create safepoint -> take snapshot -> attempt downgrade (blocked) -> follow remediation steps (roll-forward migration, rollback gate rescue).

Commits (high-level):
- Add RedwoodMigrationSafepoint CRD and controller scaffolding
- Implement safepoint persistence and manifest-hash annotation
- Add rollback-fence service and API endpoints
- Helm: pre-upgrade quiesce hook + post-rollback verifier
- Terraform: plan-lease & manifest-hash preflight check
- Postgres: timeline-aware snapshotter and WAL replay verifier
- Tests: e2e upgrade/downgrade integration and unit tests for fence logic

Changed areas (representative files):
- operator/api/v1alpha1/migration_safepoint.go
- operator/controllers/safepoint_controller.go
- installer/services/rollback_fence.go
- helm/redwood/templates/hooks/pre-upgrade-quiesce-job.yaml
- helm/redwood/templates/hooks/post-rollback-verify-job.yaml
- terraform/modules/cluster/main.tf (plan-lease check helper)
commits: b1f7a3c - scaffold RedwoodMigrationSafepoint CRD and basic controller
c3d4e58 - persist safepoint to installer metadata store and annotate releases
d9f8b21 - rollback-fence service + API and enforcement checks
e14a7c6 - helm: add pre-upgrade quiesce hook and post-rollback verify hook
f0a2b9d - terraform: manifest-hash preflight and plan-lease guard
a7b3c5d - postgres: timeline-aware snapshotter + restore verifier
9d8e6f4 - e2e: upgrade/downgrade integration test and runbook docs
review_conversation: Luis Martinez (reviewer): Can the safepoint be auto-rotated to avoid unbounded metadata growth? Suggest TTL with a configurable retention policy.
Author (Anjali): Added retention config with default 90 days and an operator GC job. See safepoint_retention_days in values.schema.json
Priya Natarajan (reviewer): The terraform plan-lease is great — what happens on CI flakes where a lease is left behind? Need a reclaim path.
Author (Anjali): Implemented a reclaim API that requires two operator approvers to force-reclaim a stale lease; documented in the runbook and protected behind RBAC. Tests added.
Ethan Zhao (reviewer): Postgres restore verifier is expensive for large tables — can we provide a fast checksum sample mode?
Author (Anjali): Added --verify-sample-percent (default 1%) to verifier and a config to whitelist critical tables for full checks. See docs.
CI Bot: Integration tests passed on run #3 (flaky network retry), all checks green.
Luis Martinez: Approving with comments. Priya: LGTM. Ethan: Approve.
source.ci_log_summary: CI pipeline: lint -> unit -> integration (e2e) -> helm-chart-tests -> terraform-plan-checks. Initial run: integration failed due to environment timeout; rerun succeeded. All checks passing on merge.
source.release_notes: Adds operator-backed migration safepoints and a rollback fence to prevent unsafe downgrades. Includes Helm pre-upgrade quiesce hooks, timeline-aware Postgres snapshotter and restore verifier, and Terraform plan-lease guard. Provides runbook and CLI verbs for interactive safepoint management.
postmerge_actions: Notify SRE and oncall to review safepoint retention defaults
Schedule canary deployment to 2 private clusters with preflight.quiesce=true
Cherry-pick terraform module changes to release/v2024-11 for customers still on older release branches
source.notes_for_support: If a customer is blocked by the rollback-fence, follow runbook: 1) request safepoint export (operatorctl safepoint list), 2) validate snapshot integrity via dry-verify, 3) either roll-forward the migration or request a two-approver force-unlock. Support should record manifest_hash and safepoint LSN when filing SUP tickets.

The restore path includes a --dry-verify mode for customers to run a low-risk restore verification before committing.

Runbook & UX: documented operator CLI verbs: safepoint create/list/delete, operatorctl fence-status, operatorctl force-unlock (requires 2 approvals). The runbook includes step-by-step rollback example: create safepoint -> take snapshot -> attempt downgrade (blocked) -> follow remediation steps (roll-forward migration, rollback gate rescue).

Commits (high-level):
- Add RedwoodMigrationSafepoint CRD and controller scaffolding
- Implement safepoint persistence and manifest-hash annotation
- Add rollback-fence service and API endpoints
- Helm: pre-upgrade quiesce hook + post-rollback verifier
- Terraform: plan-lease & manifest-hash preflight check
- Postgres: timeline-aware snapshotter and WAL replay verifier
- Tests: e2e upgrade/downgrade integration and unit tests for fence logic

Changed areas (representative files):
- operator/api/v1alpha1/migration_safepoint.go
- operator/controllers/safepoint_controller.go
- installer/services/rollback_fence.go
- helm/redwood/templates/hooks/pre-upgrade-quiesce-job.yaml
- helm/redwood/templates/hooks/post-rollback-verify-job.yaml
- terraform/modules/cluster/main.tf (plan-lease check helper)

source.title: airgap: kubelet provisioning + OCI registry mirroring + reproducible delta bundles
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: customers running fully air-gapped Private deployments reported two recurring pain points: (1) manual kubelet/node provisioning steps that vary by distro, and (2) brittle offline registry mirroring that broke during incremental upgrades. This PR delivers: 1) a cross-distro kubelet provisioning helper that generates systemd unit snippets + containerd mirror configs from the airgap catalog and injects them into the node bootstrap bundle; 2) an OCI registry mirror manifest generator that produces deterministic image manifest lists and mirror index files so mirror replication can be validated offline; 3) a reproducible delta-bundle generator that emits compact binary diffs between runtime release bundles to reduce s3/usb transfer sizes and speed upgrades in low-bandwidth datacenters; 4) an upgrade/rollback hook that stages kubelet config changes and can atomically swap systemd unit drop-ins to avoid mid-upgrade kubelet restarts. Changes are guarded behind the new installer flag --airgap-kubelet-mirror. Backwards compatibility: installers that don't enable the flag see no change in bundle layout. Safety: all generated artifacts are signed with the installer GPG key and the installer now emits a verification manifest with sha256 sums of each artifact. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for manifest generator - [x] integration smoke test with k3s and rke2 bundles - [x] installer e2e in airgapped CI matrix - [x] upgrade/rollback scenario tested on VM lab. Known limitations: kubelet helper currently supports systemd-based distros (Ubuntu/Fedora/RHEL/CentOS/Alma) and containerd; non-systemd distros will fallback to a documented manual step. See ENG-4783 for next steps (non-systemd support, Windows nodes). Deployment notes: operators must add the generated mirror index to their private registry seed; rollback is automatic only if the generated delta patch applies cleanly. Links: runway doc https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4783/airgap-kubelet-mirroring, internal S3 bucket path s3://airgap-builds/delta/2024-10-12/.
commit_summaries: a1f9e2b: installer: add kubelet-bootstrap template + systemd drop-in generation
b2c3d4e: mirror: produce OCI mirror-index.json with deterministic ordering and timestamps truncated
c3d4e5f: delta: implement binary delta emitter using xdelta3 with reproducible metadata
d4e5f6a: sign: add gpg signing step and verification manifest output
e5f6a7b: tests: add unit tests for mirror index parsing and delta apply/verify
f6a7b8c: docs: airgap user guide update for kubelet mirroring and rollback steps
review_comments: Ethan Park: The deterministic ordering in mirror-index looks good, but can you add an explicit 'build_epoch' field so downstream validators can detect reproducible builds?
Maya Chen (author): Added build_epoch as an int64 to mirror_index and updated verification manifest; kept timestamp truncation to seconds to avoid drift during replication.
Sorana Ionescu: Delta generator is a great improvement — can we ensure xdelta is bundled for Alpine? The CI runner failed on musl yesterday.
Maya Chen: Bundled a static xdelta3 in packaging/xdelta and added a small wrapper that picks the right binary for distro; CI matrix now runs alpine and debian nodes.
Diego Ramos: Concern: swapping systemd drop-ins could cause short node downtime if kubelet restarts. Add a canary swap mode and a fallback to previous drop-in on failure.

Maya Chen: Implemented staged-swap mode using atomic directory moves and a verification probe that waits for node Ready before committing; rollback path added that re-applies previous drop-in and restarts kubelet only if probe fails.
Ethan Park: Please include a short upgrade note in release notes about the new --airgap-kubelet-mirror flag and the required step to seed private registries.
Maya Chen: Added release notes and a short section in docs/airgap/kubelet-mirroring.md.
CI Bot: airgap/upgrade-e2e flaky on rke2-1.23 matrix; retried and passed on second run.
source.merge_outcome: Merged by sorana-merge-bot via squash; commit message: 'airgap: kubelet provisioning + OCI mirroring + reproducible delta bundles (ENG-4783)'.
source.release_notes: Airgapped installer: add --airgap-kubelet-mirror to generate kubelet systemd drop-ins and containerd mirror configs; produce deterministic OCI mirror-index files; produce signed reproducible delta bundles to reduce upgrade transfer size; staged swap + rollback support for kubelet unit changes. See docs/airgap/kubelet-mirroring.md for operator steps.
source.notes_for_ops: Operators must import packaging/airgap/signing/example.pub into their verification keyring and add the generated mirror-index.json to their private registry seed. For upgrades, prefer the delta bundle path to reduce transfer times; fallback to full bundle if delta apply fails. The installer will emit a verification-manifest.json alongside artifacts.
source.test_summary: Unit tests cover mirror index parsing and delta checksum verification. Integration e2e ran in airgapped CI against k3s and rke2 using VM lab; upgrade+rollback scenarios validated with a simulated 128kbps throttled link.
source.followups:
- ENG: add non-systemd node support (ENG-4890)
- Infra: publish signed xdelta binaries for more OS targets
- Docs: add operator runbook for manual recovery when delta apply fails

source.title: atomic-kms-key-promotion-with-grace-period-rollback-hooks
source.author: Elena Ruiz
source.description: Motivation: On private deployments customers run HSM-backed KMS with periodic key rotations. Past rotations that involved immediate primary key swaps caused brief failures during leader election and sealed-secrets unseal handoffs in air-gapped clusters. This PR implements an "atomic key promotion" flow plus a configurable grace period and automated rollback hooks to ensure safe cutover without downtime.
source.summary: Add atomic KMS key promotion orchestration, Helm chart hooks for staged promotion, Terraform module updates for key metadata, and rollback hooks that revert promotion state and re-issue lightweight certificates when cutover fails.
commits: 8a1f3b9: infra: add kms_promotion CRD and controller scaffolding
d2c4e7a: helm: add pre-upgrade and post-upgrade hooks for key promotion job
b6f9a11: terraform: extend redwood_kms_key resource with 'promotion_window' and 'fallback_key'
e34d2b0: installer: implement rollback hooks and safe symlink rotation for keystore
f7c2b8e: docs: document promotion lifecycle and emergency rollback steps
a9e1c44: tests: integration test for promotion graceful-fail and rollback path
review_conversation: Avery Chen (reviewer): Can you clarify the invariants the controller expects from the HSM when promoting? If the HSM can return transient errors we should not mark promotion complete.

Elena Ruiz (author): The controller treats HSM transient errors as retryable for the duration of the promotion_window. Only persistent failures (>= 3 consecutive failures over promotion_window) trigger the rollback hook. I added comments and unit tests around retry behavior.

Priya Natarajan (reviewer): Helm hooks look good but make sure the promote-posthook is idempotent in case the job is retried by Tiller or Helm3. Also include a final 'verify-pp' probe before switching primary symlink.
Elena Ruiz (author): Updated promote-posthook to use a compare-and-swap flag stored in a Kubernetes annotation; added verify-pp probe script and tests.
source.design_notes: Design highlights: 1) Introduce a small KMSPromotion CRD that records desired_key, fallback_key, state, and promotion_window. 2) Controller coordinates: pre-validate HSM key status, run promote job as Helm pre-upgrade hook, wait for verification, atomically swap primary keystore symlink, mark promotion complete. 3) On failure, controller triggers a rollback hook that restores prior symlink, re-issues short-lived certificates for workloads that request re-enrollment, and emits detailed audit events. 4) Terraform exposes promotion_window and fallback_key to ops teams to set conservative defaults for on-prem clusters.
source.backwards_compatibility: Defaults preserve prior behavior: if promotion_enabled is not set, nothing changes. The CRD is optional and controller is no-op until a KMSPromotion resource is applied.
source.rollout_plan: Phase 1: Canary in staging with one dedicated region (72h observation). Phase 2: Opt-in customers via Terraform flag 'promotion_enabled'. Phase 3: Gradual enablement with telemetry-based rollback thresholds. Ops runbook included in docs.
source.testing: Added integration e2e that simulates HSM transient failures, verifies promotion_window semantics, and asserts rollback hooks restore cluster to healthy state. Manual verification script included (scripts/tools/promote-check.sh).
source.upgrade_notes: When upgrading installer: new controller will be installed side-by-side. Operators should create KMSPromotion resources only after ensuring node agents are at vX+1. Terraform module bump required for promotion flags. No forced schema migrations.
source.rollback_instructions: If a promotion fails in production the controller will automatically invoke rollback; manual rollback steps: 1) kubectl annotate kms-promotion <name> --override=rollback=true 2) run ./scripts/tools/promote-check.sh --force-rollback 3) monitor logs (controller logs include 'rollback-complete' event)
source.release_notes:
- Added atomic KMS key promotion orchestration and controller
- Helm hooks to coordinate pre/post promotion jobs
- Terraform variables to configure promotion window and fallback keys
- Automatic rollback hooks and documented runbook for on-prem clusters
source.notes: This PR originated from a support case (SUP-8301) where a customer experienced a failed HSM promotion that took several minutes of manual intervention. The automation reduces human error and latency during cutover.
